text: part one split–apply–combine i n chapter 5 you computed average prices by filtering for each category individually — one filter call per category. that works for five categories. it does not scale to fifty, or five hundred. .groupby() solves this elegantly. the idea behind groupby is called split–apply–combine : split — divide the dataframe into groups based on the values of one column. apply — compute a statistic (mean, sum, count…) for each group independently. combine — gather the results into a new table, one row per group. python · try it — groupby mean import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], "organic": [false,true,true,false,true,true,false,false,true,true,false,true,false,true,false] } df = pd.dataframe(data) # average price per category avg_by_cat = df.groupby("category")["price"].mean() print(avg_by_cat) run clear in one line you get the mean price for every category at once. the result is a series with the category names as the index. from this alone you can already report: meat and fish are by far the most expensive categories; grains and vegetables are the most affordable.

chapter: Chapter Six — Groupby and Aggregation
text: part two different aggregations you can call any of the statistical methods from chapter 5 on a grouped series: .mean() , .median() , .sum() , .min() , .max() , .count() , .std() . python · try it import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], "organic": [false,true,true,false,true,true,false,false,true,true,false,true,false,true,false] } df = pd.dataframe(data) grouped = df.groupby("category")["price"] print("number of items per category:") print(grouped.count()) print() print("most expensive item in each category:") print(grouped.max()) print() print("total cost if you bought one of each per category:") print(grouped.sum().round(2)) run clear

chapter: Chapter Six — Groupby and Aggregation
text: part three .agg() — multiple statistics at once when you want several statistics per group simultaneously, .agg() (short for aggregate ) lets you pass a list of statistic names. the result is a clean table — perfect for a published data summary. python · try it — .agg() with a list import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], } df = pd.dataframe(data) summary = df.groupby("category")["price"].agg(["count", "mean", "median", "min", "max"]) summary = summary.round(2) print(summary) run clear this is publication-ready output. you can see at a glance that the fish category has the widest range (€1.59 to €8.99) and the meat category has the highest mean price per item. this type of table is the backbone of most data-driven food price stories. naming your columns: you can give the aggregated columns custom names inside .agg() : df.groupby("category")["price"].agg(items="count", avg="mean", max_price="max") python · try it — named aggregations import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], } df = pd.dataframe(data) summary = df.groupby("category")["price"].agg( items="count", avg_price="mean", max_price="max" ).round(2) print(summary) run clear

chapter: Chapter Six — Groupby and Aggregation
text: part four groupby multiple columns you can group by more than one column at a time by passing a list to .groupby() . the result has a multi-level index — one level per grouping column. this lets you cross-tabulate, for example: average price by category and organic status. a multiindex is just an index made of more than one label. instead of each row being identified by a single label such as dairy , it is identified by a pair such as dairy, true or dairy, false . in other words, pandas is using both category and organic to label each result row. python · try it — two-column groupby import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], "organic": [false,true,true,false,true,true,false,false,true,true,false,true,false,true,false] } df = pd.dataframe(data) # average price by category and organic status cross = df.groupby(["category", "organic"])["price"].mean().round(2) print(cross) run clear the output shows a multiindex — categories on the outer level, true/false on the inner level. for categories where only organic or only non-organic items exist (like bakery), only one row appears. this kind of cross-tabulation is extremely useful for investigating whether the organic premium varies by food category. flattening a multiindex with .reset_index() if you want an ordinary table again, the simplest method is .reset_index() . it turns the index levels back into normal columns. python · try it — flatten with reset_index() import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], "organic": [false,true,true,false,true,true,false,false,true,true,false,true,false,true,false] } df = pd.dataframe(data) cross = df.groupby(["category", "organic"])["price"].mean().round(2) flat = cross.reset_index() print(flat) run clear now category and organic are regular columns again, which is often easier to read, sort, or save. flattening by spreading one level into columns with .unstack() another method is .unstack() . it takes one index level and spreads it across the columns. here, the organic values become column headers. python · try it — flatten with unstack() import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], "organic": [false,true,true,false,true,true,false,false,true,true,false,true,false,true,false] } df = pd.dataframe(data) cross = df.groupby(["category", "organic"])["price"].mean().round(2) wide = cross.unstack() print(wide) run clear two useful patterns: use .reset_index() when you want a normal dataframe with ordinary columns. use .unstack() when you want one of the index levels to become columns.

chapter: Chapter Six — Groupby and Aggregation
text: part five your turn — category price report use .groupby() and .agg() to build a summary table that a journalist could use in a food price article. include: number of items per category, average price, and the range (max − min) per category. python · your turn import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], "organic": [false,true,true,false,true,true,false,false,true,true,false,true,false,true,false] } df = pd.dataframe(data) report = df.groupby("category")["price"].agg( items="count", avg="mean", lowest="min", highest="max" ).round(2) # add a "range" column manually report["range"] = (report["highest"] - report["lowest"]).round(2) print("=== food price summary by category ===") print(report) run clear what you learned in this chapter: the split–apply–combine concept; .groupby() with a single column and with multiple columns; computing statistics per group; using .agg() to get multiple statistics in one table; and naming aggregated columns. next chapter: sorting results and adding computed columns to your dataframe.

chapter: Chapter Seven — Sorting and New Columns
text: part one .sort_values() — ordering your data a table of prices sorted from cheapest to most expensive is far more readable than the same data in arbitrary order. .sort_values() sorts a dataframe by one or more columns. python · try it — sort ascending (cheapest first) import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], "organic": [false,true,true,false,true,true,false,false,true,true,false,true,false,true,false] } df = pd.dataframe(data) # sort by price, cheapest first (ascending is the default) df_sorted = df.sort_values("price") print(df_sorted[["item", "category", "price"]]) run clear python · try it — sort descending (most expensive first) import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], } df = pd.dataframe(data) # sort descending df_desc = df.sort_values("price", ascending=false) print(df_desc[["item", "category", "price"]].head(8)) run clear sort by multiple columns you can sort by more than one column. the first column is the primary sort key; tied rows are then broken by the second column. python · try it — sort by category, then by price import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], } df = pd.dataframe(data) df_multi = df.sort_values(["category", "price"]) print(df_multi[["category", "item", "price"]]) run clear

chapter: Chapter Seven — Sorting and New Columns
text: part two adding new columns one of the most common data analysis tasks is creating a derived column — a new column calculated from existing ones. you create it simply by assigning to a new column name. a price-per-100g column our dataset has prices for items sold in different weights. to compare fairly, we need a common unit. the data here uses fixed standard weights for illustration. the calculation happens in two steps. first, price / weight_g gives the price for 1 gram . for example, bread costs 1.29 / 400 = 0.003225 , so one gram of bread costs about €0.003225 . that number is correct, but it is awkward to read because it is so small. then we multiply by 100 to convert the result from price per 1 gram into price per 100 grams . so for bread, 0.003225 × 100 = 0.3225 , which means the bread costs about €0.3225 per 100g . that is a much easier unit for comparing foods. python · try it — calculated column import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread", "pasta", "rice", "cheese", "chicken", "salmon"], "price": [1.29, 1.19, 0.89, 3.79, 4.99, 8.99], "weight_g": [400, 500, 1000, 200, 1000, 180] } df = pd.dataframe(data) # calculate price per 100 grams df["price_per_100g"] = (df["price"] / df["weight_g"] * 100).round(3) print(df.sort_values("price_per_100g")) run clear when you sort by price_per_100g , the true value ranking is different from the raw price ranking. rice looks cheap at €0.89/kg, but so does pasta once you normalise by weight. salmon and cheese are significantly more expensive per gram — an insight hidden in the raw prices. a price-above-average flag sometimes you do not need another number. you just want a quick label that says whether each item is above the average price or not. that is what a flag does. it creates a new column of true and false values. if an item's price is greater than the mean price, the flag is true . otherwise it is false . python · try it — boolean column import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], } df = pd.dataframe(data) mean_price = df["price"].mean() df["above_average"] = df["price"] > mean_price print(f"mean price: €{mean_price:.2f}") print() print(df[["item", "price", "above_average"]]) run clear

chapter: Chapter Seven — Sorting and New Columns
text: part three .loc[] — classifying prices into bands sometimes you want to turn numeric values into labels — for example, grouping prices into bands such as "budget", "mid-range", and "premium". you can do this with a new column plus a few .loc[] assignments. python · try it import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], } df = pd.dataframe(data) # start by assuming every item is premium df["band"] = "premium" # overwrite rows that match cheaper price ranges df.loc[df["price"] run clear order matters: start with the broadest default label, then overwrite it with narrower conditions. here we begin with "premium" , then replace some rows with "mid-range" , then replace the cheapest rows with "budget" .

chapter: Chapter Seven — Sorting and New Columns
text: part four string methods with .str pandas exposes python string methods on text columns through the .str accessor. this lets you clean, search, and transform text without writing loops. method what it does example .str.upper() converts text to uppercase. "olive oil" becomes "olive oil" . .str.lower() converts text to lowercase. "milk" becomes "milk" . .str.len() counts how many characters each string has. "olive oil" has length 9 . .str.contains("...") checks whether text contains a pattern. .str.contains(" ") finds names with a space, such as "olive oil" . .str.startswith("...") checks whether text begins with certain characters. .str.startswith("m") matches "milk" . .str.endswith("...") checks whether text ends with certain characters. .str.endswith("d") matches "bread" . .str.replace(old, new) replaces one piece of text with another. "olive oil" can become "olive-oil" . .str.strip() removes spaces from the beginning and end of text. " milk " becomes "milk" . what does regex=true mean? it tells pandas to treat the search text as a pattern , not as ordinary text. for example, df["item"].str.contains("^b", regex=true) means “find names that start with the letter b”, so it matches "bread" and "butter" . if you want pandas to look for the exact characters only, use regex=false . python · try it import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], } df = pd.dataframe(data) # convert item names to uppercase print("uppercase names:") print(df["item"].str.upper().head()) print() # find items whose name contains more than one word multi_word = df[df["item"].str.contains(" ")] print("items with multi-word names:") print(multi_word[["item", "price"]]) print() # length of each item name df["name_length"] = df["item"].str.len() print("name lengths:") print(df[["item", "name_length"]].sort_values("name_length", ascending=false)) run clear

chapter: Chapter Seven — Sorting and New Columns
partTitle: Your Turn — Build a Price Index
text: part five your turn — build a price index create a "value score" column that divides each item's price by the overall mean price. a score below 1.0 means the item is cheaper than average; above 1.0 means it is more expensive. sort the result from best value to worst. python · your turn import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread","milk","eggs","cheese","butter","olive oil","pasta","rice","tomatoes","onions","potatoes","chicken","beef","salmon","tuna"], "category": ["bakery","dairy","dairy","dairy","dairy","oils","grains","grains","vegetables","vegetables","vegetables","meat","meat","fish","fish"], "price": [1.29,0.99,2.49,3.79,1.89,5.49,1.19,0.89,1.49,0.69,0.79,4.99,7.49,8.99,1.59], } df = pd.dataframe(data) mean_price = df["price"].mean() df["value_score"] = (df["price"] / mean_price).round(2) result = df[["item", "category", "price", "value_score"]].sort_values("value_score") print(f"(mean price used as baseline: €{mean_price:.2f})") print() print(result) run clear what you learned in this chapter: sorting with .sort_values() by one or multiple columns; creating new columns via arithmetic on existing ones; classifying values into categories with .loc[] and boolean conditions; and using .str methods to work with text columns. in the final chapter, you will learn to handle missing data and put all these skills together in a complete analysis.

chapter: Chapter Eight — Cleaning and Analysis
text: part one detecting missing values i n the real world, datasets are never perfect. a price might be missing because a product was out of stock. a category might be blank because it was not recorded. pandas represents missing values as nan (not a number), and it provides methods to find and handle them. python · try it — a messy dataset import pandas as pd import numpy as np # for creating nan values data = { "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "cheese", "butter", "olive oil", "pasta", "rice"], "category": ["bakery", "dairy", none, "dairy", "dairy", none, "grains", "grains"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, np.nan, 3.79, 1.89, 5.49, np.nan, 0.89], "organic": [false, true, true, false, true, true, false, false] } df = pd.dataframe(data) print("dataset with missing values:") print(df) print() print("missing values per column:") print(df.isnull().sum()) run clear .isnull() returns a boolean dataframe where true marks every missing value. calling .sum() on it counts the true values per column — giving you a quick inventory of how much data is missing. this is always one of the first checks in any real-world analysis. python · try it — find rows with any missing value import pandas as pd import numpy as np data = { "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "cheese", "butter", "olive oil", "pasta", "rice"], "category": ["bakery", "dairy", none, "dairy", "dairy", none, "grains", "grains"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, np.nan, 3.79, 1.89, 5.49, np.nan, 0.89], "organic": [false, true, true, false, true, true, false, false] } df = pd.dataframe(data) # rows with at least one missing value rows_with_missing = df[df.isnull().any(axis=1)] print("rows with missing values:") print(rows_with_missing) run clear

chapter: Chapter Eight — Cleaning and Analysis
text: part two .dropna() and .fillna() — handling missing data once you know where the missing values are, you have two main options: drop the affected rows, or fill in the missing values with a substitute. .dropna() — remove rows with missing values python · try it — drop rows with any nan import pandas as pd import numpy as np data = { "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "cheese", "butter", "olive oil", "pasta", "rice"], "category": ["bakery", "dairy", none, "dairy", "dairy", none, "grains", "grains"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, np.nan, 3.79, 1.89, 5.49, np.nan, 0.89], "organic": [false, true, true, false, true, true, false, false] } df = pd.dataframe(data) print(f"rows before: {df.shape[0]}") df_clean = df.dropna() print(f"rows after dropna: {df_clean.shape[0]}") print() print(df_clean) run clear .fillna() — replace missing values sometimes dropping rows loses too much data. .fillna() replaces nan with a value you specify — the mean, a fixed default, or a placeholder string. python · try it — fill missing values import pandas as pd import numpy as np data = { "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "cheese", "butter", "olive oil", "pasta", "rice"], "category": ["bakery", "dairy", none, "dairy", "dairy", none, "grains", "grains"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, np.nan, 3.79, 1.89, 5.49, np.nan, 0.89], "organic": [false, true, true, false, true, true, false, false] } df = pd.dataframe(data) # fill missing prices with the mean price mean_price = df["price"].mean() df["price"] = df["price"].fillna(mean_price) # fill missing categories with "unknown" df["category"] = df["category"].fillna("unknown") print(f"mean price used to fill gaps: €{mean_price:.2f}") print() print(df) run clear choosing a strategy: dropping rows is safe when missing data is rare and random. filling with the mean is common for prices, but it can distort analysis if many values are missing. always document which strategy you chose and why — it is part of your methodology.

chapter: Chapter Eight — Cleaning and Analysis
text: part three renaming columns and removing duplicates .rename() — fix column names python · try it import pandas as pd data = { "item name": ["bread", "milk", "eggs"], "price €": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49], "cat": ["bakery", "dairy", "dairy"] } df = pd.dataframe(data) print("before rename:") print(df.columns.tolist()) df = df.rename(columns={ "item name": "item", "price €": "price", "cat": "category" }) print("after rename:") print(df.columns.tolist()) print() print(df) run clear .drop_duplicates() — remove repeated rows python · try it import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread", "milk", "bread", "eggs", "milk"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, 1.29, 2.49, 0.99] } df = pd.dataframe(data) print(f"rows before deduplication: {df.shape[0]}") df_unique = df.drop_duplicates() print(f"rows after deduplication: {df_unique.shape[0]}") print() print(df_unique) run clear

chapter: Chapter Eight — Cleaning and Analysis
partTitle: Putting It All Together — A Complete Food Price Analysis
text: part four putting it all together — a complete food price analysis you now have all the tools needed to conduct a full data analysis. below is a complete, commented script that takes a raw (and slightly messy) price survey dataset, cleans it, and produces a set of findings you could publish. python · complete analysis import pandas as pd import numpy as np # ── 1. load the raw data ────────────────────────────────────────── raw = { "item": [ "bread", "milk", "eggs", "cheese", "butter", "olive oil", "pasta", "rice", "tomatoes", "onions", "potatoes", "chicken", "beef", "salmon", "tuna", "bread" # duplicate entry ], "category": [ "bakery", "dairy", "dairy", "dairy", "dairy", "oils", "grains", "grains", "vegetables", "vegetables", "vegetables", "meat", "meat", "fish", "fish", "bakery" ], "price": [ 1.29, 0.99, np.nan, 3.79, 1.89, 5.49, 1.19, 0.89, 1.49, 0.69, 0.79, 4.99, 7.49, 8.99, 1.59, 1.29 ], "organic": [ false, true, true, false, true, true, false, false, true, true, false, true, false, true, false, false ] } df = pd.dataframe(raw) print(f"raw dataset: {df.shape[0]} rows, {df.shape[1]} columns") print(f"missing values: {df.isnull().sum().sum()}") print(f"duplicate rows: {df.duplicated().sum()}") # ── 2. clean ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── # fill missing prices with the median of their category category_medians = df.groupby("category")["price"].median() df.loc[(df["category"] == "dairy") & (df["price"].isnull()), "price"] = category_medians["dairy"] df.loc[(df["category"] == "grains") & (df["price"].isnull()), "price"] = category_medians["grains"] # remove duplicates df = df.drop_duplicates() print(f"\nclean dataset: {df.shape[0]} rows") # ── 3. enrich ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── # add a price band column df["band"] = "premium" df.loc[df["price"] run clear read through the script section by section. notice how each step builds on the previous one: load → clean → enrich → analyse. this is the standard structure of any data analysis pipeline, whether you are working with 15 rows or 15 million.

chapter: Chapter Eight — Cleaning and Analysis
text:
part five project — your own price survey build a complete analysis of your own food price dataset. walk to a local supermarket or browse an online grocery store and record the prices of 15–20 items. then, using the full toolkit from this book, answer: what are the three most expensive categories? what is the organic premium in your market? which items represent the best value (lowest price-to-median ratio)? are there any suspicious outliers in your data? python · your project — replace the data with your own import pandas as pd # replace this with your own survey data my_data = { "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "cheese", "tomatoes", "chicken", "pasta", "rice", "olive oil", "yoghurt"], "category": ["bakery", "dairy", "dairy", "dairy", "vegetables", "meat", "grains", "grains", "oils", "dairy"], "price": [1.49, 1.09, 2.79, 4.29, 1.69, 5.49, 1.39, 0.99, 6.29, 0.89], "organic": [false, true, false, false, true, false, false, false, true, true] } df = pd.dataframe(my_data) # --- your analysis below --- print("=== my market price survey ===
") print("dataset overview:") print(f" {df.shape[0]} items across {df['category'].nunique()} categories
") print("price summary:") print(f" cheapest: {df.loc[df['price'].idxmin(), 'item']} (€{df['price'].min():.2f})") print(f" priciest: {df.loc[df['price'].idxmax(), 'item']} (€{df['price'].max():.2f})") print(f" mean: €{df['price'].mean():.2f}") print(f" median: €{df['price'].median():.2f}
") print("average price by category:") cat_summary = df.groupby("category")["price"].mean().sort_values(ascending=false).round(2) print(cat_summary.to_string()) print() organic_avg = df[df["organic"]]["price"].mean() nonorganic_avg = df[~df["organic"]]["price"].mean() print(f"organic premium: €{(organic_avg - nonorganic_avg):.2f}") run clear you now have a full analysis workflow. you can load, inspect, clean, filter, aggregate, sort, and enrich tabular data using pandas. in part six you will learn how to combine multiple dataframes — an essential skill whenever your data arrives in separate files or tables.

chapter: Chapter Eight — Cleaning and Analysis
partTitle: Combining DataFrames — concat, merge, and join
text: part six combining dataframes — concat, merge, and join r eal projects rarely live in a single table. you might have price data from two different supermarkets in separate files, or product details in one table and prices in another. pandas gives you three main tools for combining dataframes: pd.concat() stacks them, pd.merge() joins them on a shared column, and .join() joins them on their index. pd.concat() — stack dataframes pd.concat() glues dataframes together end-to-end. by default it stacks rows (adding more observations). pass axis=1 to stack columns side by side instead. python · try it — stacking two surveys import pandas as pd # survey from supermarket a store_a = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49], "store": ["a", "a", "a"] }) # survey from supermarket b store_b = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "butter"], "price": [1.39, 1.09, 1.99], "store": ["b", "b", "b"] }) # stack them into one dataframe combined = pd.concat([store_a, store_b], ignore_index=true) print(combined) run clear ignore_index=true resets the row numbers in the result so you get a clean 0, 1, 2 … sequence instead of the original indexes repeating. without it, row 0 would appear twice — once from each source dataframe. pd.merge() — join on a shared column pd.merge() works like a sql join: it matches rows from two dataframes based on a shared key column. the how parameter controls which rows survive: inner (default) — only rows that appear in both tables. left — all rows from the left table; unmatched rows from the right become nan . right — all rows from the right table. outer — all rows from both tables. python · try it — inner merge import pandas as pd # product details products = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "cheese"], "category": ["bakery", "dairy", "dairy", "dairy"], "organic": [false, true, true, false] }) # prices from a survey prices = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["milk", "eggs", "butter", "bread"], "price": [0.99, 2.49, 1.89, 1.29] }) # inner merge — only items found in both tables merged = pd.merge(products, prices, on="item") print("inner merge (items in both tables):") print(merged) run clear python · try it — left merge (keep all products) import pandas as pd products = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "cheese"], "category": ["bakery", "dairy", "dairy", "dairy"], "organic": [false, true, true, false] }) prices = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["milk", "eggs", "butter", "bread"], "price": [0.99, 2.49, 1.89, 1.29] }) # left merge — keep all products; cheese gets nan for price merged_left = pd.merge(products, prices, on="item", how="left") print("left merge (all products, nan where no price):") print(merged_left) run clear df.join() — join on the index .join() is a shortcut for merging on the dataframe index rather than a named column. it is handy when both dataframes share the same index — for example, after you set the item name as the index. python · try it — join by index import pandas as pd # set "item" as the index on each dataframe details = pd.dataframe({ "category": ["bakery", "dairy", "dairy"], "organic": [false, true, true] }, index=["bread", "milk", "eggs"]) prices = pd.dataframe({ "price": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49] }, index=["bread", "milk", "eggs"]) # join matches rows by their shared index result = details.join(prices) print(result) run clear when to use which: use pd.concat() when you have multiple dataframes with the same columns and want to stack them. use pd.merge() when two tables share a key column and you want to combine their columns — this is the everyday workhorse. use .join() as a convenience when you have already set a meaningful index.

chapter: Chapter Eight — Cleaning and Analysis
text: part seven string methods — .str.contains(), .str.extract(), .str.split() t ext columns in real datasets are rarely clean. product names arrive with extra words, codes, or units embedded inside them. pandas exposes python's regex engine directly through the .str accessor, so you can search, extract, and split text across an entire column in one line. .str.contains() — filter rows by pattern .str.contains(pattern) returns a boolean series — true wherever the pattern matches. you use it exactly like any other boolean filter. python · try it — find items with "oil" or "oil" in the name import pandas as pd df = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["olive oil", "sunflower oil", "bread", "milk", "coconut oil", "eggs"], "price": [5.49, 3.29, 1.29, 0.99, 4.79, 2.49] }) # regex=true is the default; na=false treats nan cells as non-matching oils = df[df["item"].str.contains("oil", case=false, regex=true, na=false)] print(oils) run clear python · try it — regex pattern: items that start with a vowel import pandas as pd df = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["olive oil", "eggs", "apples", "bread", "onions", "milk"], "price": [5.49, 2.49, 1.99, 1.29, 0.69, 0.99] }) # ^ anchors to start of string; na=false so any nan rows are excluded starts_vowel = df[df["item"].str.contains(r"^[aeiouaeiou]", na=false)] print(starts_vowel) run clear .str.extract() — pull a pattern out into a new column .str.extract(pattern) applies a regex with a capture group ( ) and returns the captured text as a new column. it only captures the first match per cell. python · try it — extract the unit from a combined price string import pandas as pd # raw data where price and unit are stuck together df = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["olive oil", "milk", "pasta", "rice"], "price_raw": ["5.49€/l", "0.99€/l", "1.19€/kg", "0.89€/kg"] }) # capture group (\w+) grabs the unit after the slash df["unit"] = df["price_raw"].str.extract(r"/(\w+)") # capture group ([\d.]+) grabs the numeric price df["price"] = df["price_raw"].str.extract(r"([\d.]+)").astype(float) print(df) run clear python · try it — extractall() captures every match, not just the first import pandas as pd # each cell contains multiple codes separated by commas df = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "olive oil"], "codes": ["bio-12, local-07", "pdo-03, org-11, local-02"] }) # extractall returns one row per match; use a capture group matches = df["codes"].str.extractall(r"([a-z]+-\d+)") print(matches) run clear .str.split() — break a string into parts .str.split(pattern) splits each string on a delimiter and returns a list. add expand=true to spread the parts into separate columns automatically. python · try it — split a "category / subcategory" column import pandas as pd df = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["full-fat milk", "greek yoghurt", "cheddar cheese"], "cat_raw": ["dairy / milk", "dairy / yoghurt", "dairy / cheese"] }) # split on " / " and expand into two columns df[["category", "subcategory"]] = df["cat_raw"].str.split(" / ", expand=true) print(df[["item", "category", "subcategory"]]) run clear other useful .str methods python · try it — strip, lower, replace import pandas as pd df = pd.dataframe({ "item": [" bread ", "milk", "eggs", " olive oil "] }) # strip whitespace, then lowercase everything df["item_clean"] = df["item"].str.strip().str.lower() # replace spaces with underscores (useful for column-safe names) df["item_slug"] = df["item_clean"].str.replace(r"\s+", "_", regex=true) print(df) run clear two parameters to always remember: pass na=false to .str.contains() whenever your column might have missing values — without it, nan cells return nan instead of false , which breaks boolean filters. pass regex=true to make the intent explicit; it is the default for .str.contains() and .str.extract() , but must be set explicitly on .str.replace() in newer pandas versions. the .str accessor chains freely: df["col"].str.strip().str.lower().str.replace(…) all in one expression.

chapter: Chapter Eight — Cleaning and Analysis
partTitle: Wide Data vs Long Data — and How to Convert Between Them
text: part eight wide data vs long data — and how to convert between them i magine you are tracking the price of five grocery items across three months. there are two completely different ways to arrange that information in a table, and the choice has enormous consequences for what you can do with the data afterwards. what is wide data? in a wide table, each subject (here, each grocery item) occupies exactly one row, and every measurement gets its own column. the table literally grows wider as you add more measurements. python · see it — a wide price table import pandas as pd # wide format: one row per item, one column per month. # a human reading this spreadsheet can instantly compare # january vs february vs march for any given item. wide = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "olive oil", "pasta"], "jan": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49, 5.49, 1.19], "feb": [1.35, 0.99, 2.59, 5.49, 1.19], "mar": [1.39, 1.09, 2.79, 5.99, 1.29], }) print(wide) run clear wide format is natural for humans. a journalist glancing at that table immediately sees that olive oil jumped from €5.49 to €5.99. it is the format that excel spreadsheets, government statistical publications, and most printed reports use. it is compact — five items across three months fit in five rows and four columns. what is long data? in a long table, each individual measurement gets its own row. instead of spreading months across columns, you add a variable column (which month?) and a value column (what was the price?). the table grows taller as you add more measurements — five items across three months produce fifteen rows. python · see it — the same data in long format import pandas as pd # long format: one row per observation (one item on one month). # every row answers three questions: which item? which month? what price? # notice: 5 items × 3 months = 15 rows. long = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread","bread","bread", "milk", "milk", "milk", "eggs", "eggs", "eggs", "olive oil","olive oil","olive oil", "pasta","pasta","pasta"], "month": ["jan","feb","mar", "jan","feb","mar", "jan","feb","mar", "jan","feb","mar", "jan","feb","mar"], "price": [1.29,1.35,1.39, 0.99,0.99,1.09, 2.49,2.59,2.79, 5.49,5.49,5.99, 1.19,1.19,1.29], }) print(long) run clear why would anyone use wide format? wide format wins whenever a human needs to read the table directly: side-by-side comparison. it is much easier to scan a row and compare january vs february vs march than to hunt through fifteen rows. compact reporting. a published table with five rows fits on one page. a fifteen-row table does not. spreadsheet formulas. excel and google sheets functions like sum(b2:d2) assume your data is wide. certain statistical software. some older tools (spss, certain r functions) expect one row per subject. data entry. it is faster and less error-prone for a human to fill in one row per item than to create a new row for every month. why would anyone use long format? long format wins whenever a computer needs to process the data: groupby and aggregation. df.groupby("month")["price"].mean() works instantly on long data. on wide data, you would have to select columns by name and write separate logic for each month — and adding a fourth month means rewriting your code. filtering. df[df["month"] == "feb"] is one line. filtering wide data for a specific month means knowing and referencing an exact column name. plotting. seaborn, plotly, and most visualisation libraries expect long format. they need a single column to map to the x-axis, a single column for y, and a single column for colour — wide data cannot provide that cleanly. scalability. if you add a fourth month to long data, you add five rows. if you add a fourth month to wide data, you add a whole new column — which breaks every formula, query, and chart that referenced column positions. databases and sql. relational databases are built around long format. a sql query like group by month requires a column called month . the rule of thumb: collect and store data in long format. reshape it to wide format only at the last step, when you need to present it to a human reader. this is the same principle as keeping a database normalised and only denormalising for a report. pd.melt() — turning wide into long pd.melt() is the standard pandas function for unpivoting a wide table into long format. you tell it which columns are identifiers (the columns that stay fixed — here, item ) and which columns are values to be melted down into rows (here, jan , feb , mar ). it creates two new columns: one holding the old column names and one holding the old cell values. python · pd.melt() — wide to long import pandas as pd wide = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "olive oil", "pasta"], "jan": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49, 5.49, 1.19], "feb": [1.35, 0.99, 2.59, 5.49, 1.19], "mar": [1.39, 1.09, 2.79, 5.99, 1.29], }) long = pd.melt( wide, # id_vars: columns that identify the subject. # these are not melted — they repeat once per measurement. id_vars="item", # value_vars: columns that hold measurements to be melted. # each column name becomes a row in the new "month" column. # if you omit value_vars, every non-id column is melted. value_vars=["jan", "feb", "mar"], # var_name: what to call the new column that holds # the old column names ("jan", "feb", "mar"). var_name="month", # value_name: what to call the new column that holds # the actual cell values (the prices). value_name="price", ) # sort so we can read item by item long = long.sort_values(["item", "month"]).reset_index(drop=true) print(long) run clear now that the data is long, all of pandas' analytical tools work naturally. here is why that matters in practice: python · why long format pays off — groupby on the melted data import pandas as pd wide = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "olive oil", "pasta"], "jan": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49, 5.49, 1.19], "feb": [1.35, 0.99, 2.59, 5.49, 1.19], "mar": [1.39, 1.09, 2.79, 5.99, 1.29], }) long = pd.melt(wide, id_vars="item", value_vars=["jan", "feb", "mar"], var_name="month", value_name="price") # question 1: what was the average basket price each month? # on wide data this would require manually averaging three different columns. # on long data it is one line that works regardless of how many months you have. avg_by_month = long.groupby("month")["price"].mean().round(2) print("average price by month:") print(avg_by_month) print() # question 2: which item had the biggest price rise across the period? item_min = long.groupby("item")["price"].min() item_max = long.groupby("item")["price"].max() rise = (item_max - item_min).sort_values(ascending=false).round(2) print("price rise per item (max minus min):") print(rise) run clear df.pivot() — turning long back into wide .pivot() is the reverse operation. you give it three arguments: the column whose unique values become the new row index, the column whose unique values become the new column headers, and the column that fills the cells. python · df.pivot() — long back to wide import pandas as pd # starting from long format (as you would have it after analysis) long = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread","bread","bread","milk","milk","milk","eggs","eggs","eggs"], "month": ["jan","feb","mar","jan","feb","mar","jan","feb","mar"], "price": [1.29,1.35,1.39, 0.99,0.99,1.09, 2.49,2.59,2.79], }) wide = long.pivot( # index: the column whose values become row labels. # each unique item gets one row. index="item", # columns: the column whose values become column headers. # each unique month gets one column. columns="month", # values: the column that fills the cells at each (item, month) intersection. values="price", ) # pivot() keeps the column axis name ("month") as a label above the headers. # removing it makes the table look like a plain report. wide.columns.name = none print(wide) run clear pivot() vs pivot_table(): .pivot() requires that the combination of index and columns is unique — one price per (item, month) pair. if your data has duplicates (e.g. two price readings for the same item and month), .pivot() will raise an error. use pd.pivot_table() instead, which accepts an aggfunc argument (e.g. aggfunc="mean" ) to collapse duplicates automatically.

chapter: Chapter Eight — Cleaning and Analysis
partTitle: Applying Your Own Functions to a DataFrame
text: part nine applying your own functions to a dataframe p andas built-in methods cover a lot of ground, but sooner or later you will need logic that does not exist as a ready-made function. the .apply() method bridges that gap: it calls any python function you write — once per cell, once per row, or once per column — and collects the results back into the dataframe. the problem: messy item names from the real world scraped product names are rarely clean. the same item might arrive as " ελαιόλαδο " , "ελαιόλαδο" , or "elaiolado (extra virgin)" depending on the source. before you can group, merge, or compare, you need every name reduced to a single canonical form. this is exactly the kind of multi-step logic that belongs in a def . python · define normalize_string() import unicodedata import re def normalize_string(text): """ turn any messy product name into a clean, comparable slug. steps: strip → lowercase → remove accents → keep only letters/digits/spaces → collapse spaces. """ # guard: if the value is not a string (e.g. nan, a number), return it unchanged. # this prevents a crash when the column has missing values. if not isinstance(text, str): return text # step 1 — strip leading and trailing whitespace. # " olive oil " → "olive oil" text = text.strip() # step 2 — lowercase everything so "milk" and "milk" are treated as the same item. text = text.lower() # step 3 — decompose accented characters into base letter + combining accent mark, # then discard every combining mark (category "mn" = mark, nonspacing). # "elaiólaðo" → "elaiolado" # without this step, "αθήνα" and "αθηνα" would sort as different strings. text = unicodedata.normalize("nfd", text) text = "".join(ch for ch in text if unicodedata.category(ch) != "mn") # step 4 — strip anything that is not a letter, a digit, or a space. # removes parentheses, dashes, asterisks, and other punctuation that # would make two otherwise identical names fail to match. text = re.sub(r"[^\w\s]", "", text) # step 5 — collapse runs of whitespace (spaces, tabs) into a single space. # "olive oil" → "olive oil" text = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text).strip() return text # test the function on individual strings before applying it to a whole column test_cases = [ " ελαιόλαδο ", "olive oil (extra virgin)", "γάλα πλήρες", "feta-cheese*", 42, # non-string — should pass through unchanged ] for t in test_cases: print(repr(t), "→", repr(normalize_string(t))) run clear always test a function on a handful of representative values before unleashing it on thousands of rows. the repr() calls above show the exact string boundaries so you can spot stray spaces or invisible characters that a plain print() would hide. series.apply() — one column at a time series.apply(func) calls func once for each value in the column and returns a new series of the same length. it is the simplest and most common form of .apply() . python · apply normalize_string() to a column import pandas as pd import unicodedata import re def normalize_string(text): if not isinstance(text, str): return text text = text.strip() text = text.lower() text = unicodedata.normalize("nfd", text) text = "".join(ch for ch in text if unicodedata.category(ch) != "mn") text = re.sub(r"[^\w\s]", "", text) text = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text).strip() return text # realistic messy data: same items from three different sources df = pd.dataframe({ "raw_name": [ " ελαιόλαδο ", "olive oil (extra virgin)", "γάλα πλήρες", "milk (full-fat)*", "feta-cheese", "φετα", ], "price": [5.49, 5.99, 0.99, 1.09, 3.79, 4.29] }) # .apply() passes each value in "raw_name" into normalize_string() one at a time. # the result is a new series that we store as a new column. df["name_clean"] = df["raw_name"].apply(normalize_string) print(df[["raw_name", "name_clean", "price"]]) run clear dataframe.apply() with axis=1 — one row at a time when you pass axis=1 , pandas calls your function once per row , passing the entire row as a series. this lets you combine values from multiple columns in one step — something series.apply() cannot do alone. python · axis=1 — build a display label from two columns import pandas as pd import unicodedata import re def normalize_string(text): if not isinstance(text, str): return text text = text.strip() text = text.lower() text = unicodedata.normalize("nfd", text) text = "".join(ch for ch in text if unicodedata.category(ch) != "mn") text = re.sub(r"[^\w\s]", "", text) text = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text).strip() return text df = pd.dataframe({ "item": [" ελαιόλαδο ", "milk (full-fat)*", "feta-cheese"], "store": [" ab vassilopoulos", "sklavenitis ", " lidl"], "price": [5.49, 1.09, 3.79] }) # axis=1 passes a whole row to the function. # row["item"] and row["store"] are individual values from that row. def make_key(row): clean_item = normalize_string(row["item"]) clean_store = normalize_string(row["store"]) # combine into a single lookup key: "elaiolado | ab vassilopoulos" return f"{clean_item} | {clean_store}" df["lookup_key"] = df.apply(make_key, axis=1) print(df[["item", "store", "lookup_key"]]) run clear lambda vs def — when to use which a lambda is an anonymous function — a function with no name, written in a single expression instead of a full def block. the syntax is: lambda argument : expression the word lambda replaces def . everything to the left of the colon is the parameter list. everything to the right is the return value — no return keyword needed, because a lambda always returns the result of that one expression. so lambda p: round(p * 1.24, 2) is exactly the same as writing: def apply_vat(p): return round(p * 1.24, 2) the difference is that a lambda is created inline, at the point where it is used, and then discarded. it has no name you can call elsewhere in your code, and it cannot contain more than one expression — no if/elif chains, no loops, no multiple statements. that limitation is also its strength: when you read .apply(lambda p: round(p * 1.24, 2)) , the entire logic is visible right there in the line, without having to scroll up to find the function definition. for very short transformations — one expression, no guard clause — a lambda avoids the overhead of naming a function. for anything with more than one step, a named def is clearer and easier to test. python · lambda for simple transforms, def for complex ones import pandas as pd df = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "olive oil"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49, 5.49], }) # lambda is fine here — one expression, no branching df["price_with_vat"] = df["price"].apply(lambda p: round(p * 1.24, 2)) # for anything with conditionals or multiple steps, # a named def is easier to read and to debug def price_band(price): if price run clear series.map() — functions, dictionaries, and lookup tables .map() works element-wise on a single series, exactly like series.apply() when you pass it a function. the critical difference is that .map() also accepts a dictionary or another series as its argument, turning it into a vectorised lookup table — something .apply() cannot do. python · map with a function — identical to series.apply() import pandas as pd df = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "olive oil"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49, 5.49], }) # passing a function: map calls it once per value, just like apply. # for element-wise work on a single column, map and apply are interchangeable. df["price_rounded"] = df["price"].map(lambda p: round(p * 1.24, 2)) print(df) run clear python · map with a dictionary — the feature apply does not have import pandas as pd df = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "olive oil", "pasta"], "category": ["bakery", "dairy", "dairy", "oils", "grains"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49, 5.49, 1.19], }) # a lookup table that maps each category to a vat rate. # this is far more readable than a chain of if/elif statements. vat_rates = { "bakery": 0.06, "dairy": 0.06, "oils": 0.13, "grains": 0.06, } # .map(dict) looks up each value in the "category" column as a key. # any value not in the dict becomes nan — useful for spotting unmapped categories. df["vat_rate"] = df["category"].map(vat_rates) # price * (1 + vat_rate) is the standard "gross-up" formula. # multiplying by 1 adds the original price; multiplying by vat_rate adds the tax. # e.g. bread: 1.29 * (1 + 0.06) = 1.29 * 1.06 = 1.3674 → rounded to 1.37 # this is equivalent to writing price + (price * vat_rate), but shorter. df["price_with_vat"] = (df["price"] * (1 + df["vat_rate"])).round(2) print(df) run clear the nan-for-unmatched behaviour is a feature, not a bug. after the .map() call, df["vat_rate"].isnull().sum() instantly tells you how many rows have a category your lookup table does not recognise — a natural data-quality check. dataframe.map() — apply a function to every cell dataframe.map() (added in pandas 2.1, replacing the older applymap() ) calls a function on every individual cell in the entire dataframe. use it when you want to transform all values in one pass — for example, rounding every number in a table of prices. python · dataframe.map() — round every numeric cell import pandas as pd # a price comparison table across three stores prices = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs"], "ab": [1.2934, 0.9901, 2.4870], "skl": [1.3499, 1.0850, 2.5000], "lidl": [1.1990, 0.9450, 2.3999], }) # select only the numeric columns (the store columns) # and apply dataframe.map() to round every cell to 2 decimal places. # dataframe.map() calls the function once per cell, left to right, top to bottom. store_cols = ["ab", "skl", "lidl"] prices[store_cols] = prices[store_cols].map(lambda x: round(x, 2)) print(prices) run clear choosing between apply, map, and map(dict): use series.apply(func) or series.map(func) interchangeably for element-wise work on one column — they produce the same result. reach for series.map(dict) whenever you need a lookup table; it is faster and more readable than an equivalent if/elif chain inside .apply() . use dataframe.apply(axis=1) when your function needs values from more than one column. use dataframe.map(func) when you want to transform every cell in the table at once. a note on speed: .apply() calls your python function in a loop, one value at a time. for very large datasets this can be slow. if a vectorised pandas method exists — .str.lower() , .str.strip() , arithmetic operators — prefer it over .apply() . use .apply() when you need logic that pandas cannot express natively: multi-step conditionals, external lookups, or anything that requires a full python function.

chapter: Chapter Nine — Reading and Writing CSV Files
text: part one what is a csv file? c sv stands for comma-separated values . it is one of the simplest ways to store table data in a file. each line is a row. each comma separates one column from the next. when you work with pandas on real projects, a very common workflow is: read a csv → analyse the data → write a new csv . that is how you move data between spreadsheets, scripts, and reports. path what it means "prices.csv" a file named prices.csv in the same folder as your script or notebook. "data/prices.csv" a file inside a subfolder called data . "c:/users/ana/data/prices.csv" or "/users/ana/data/prices.csv" an absolute path: the full location of the file on your computer. keep paths simple: when you are starting out, it is easiest to save your csv in the same folder as your script and use a path like "prices.csv" .

chapter: Chapter Nine — Reading and Writing CSV Files
text: part two reading a csv with pd.read_csv() pd.read_csv("prices.csv") tells pandas: “open the file called prices.csv and turn it into a dataframe.” in this playground, we create the file first so the example can run. on your own computer, the file would usually already exist. python · try it — read a csv import pandas as pd sample = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "cheese"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49, 3.79], "category": ["bakery", "dairy", "dairy", "dairy"] }) # save a demo csv file so we have something to read sample.to_csv("prices.csv", index=false) # read the csv back into pandas df = pd.read_csv("prices.csv") print(df) run clear the result is a normal dataframe. once the file is loaded, you can use everything you already know: .head() , filtering, sorting, groupby() , new columns, and so on.

chapter: Chapter Nine — Reading and Writing CSV Files
text: part three writing a csv with .to_csv() to save a dataframe to a csv file, use .to_csv("filename.csv") . the filename is the path where you want the file to be written. the option index=false is important. without it, pandas also writes the row numbers as an extra first column. most of the time, that is not what you want in a csv you plan to share or reopen later. python · try it — write a csv import pandas as pd data = { "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "cheese", "butter", "rice"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49, 3.79, 1.89, 0.89], "category": ["bakery", "dairy", "dairy", "dairy", "dairy", "grains"] } df = pd.dataframe(data) cheap = df[df["price"] run clear rule of thumb: use index=false unless you deliberately want the row numbers saved into the csv.

chapter: Chapter Nine — Reading and Writing CSV Files
text: part four a full csv workflow here is the complete pattern you will use in real work: load a csv, analyse it, then save the result as a new csv. python · try it — read, analyse, write import pandas as pd sample = pd.dataframe({ "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "cheese", "rice", "tomatoes"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49, 3.79, 0.89, 1.49], "category": ["bakery", "dairy", "dairy", "dairy", "grains", "vegetables"] }) sample.to_csv("prices.csv", index=false) df = pd.read_csv("prices.csv") summary = df.groupby("category")["price"].mean().round(2).reset_index() summary.to_csv("category_summary.csv", index=false) print("summary table:") print(summary) print() print("saved csv read back in:") print(pd.read_csv("category_summary.csv")) run clear notice the flow: prices.csv comes in, pandas creates a summary table, and then that summary is written to category_summary.csv . this is how you build outputs that editors, classmates, or other scripts can reuse.

chapter: Chapter Nine — Reading and Writing CSV Files
partTitle: Your Turn — Save and Reload Your Own Data
text: part five your turn — save and reload your own data create a small price table of your own, save it to a csv, read it back in, and then save a filtered version. try to produce two files: my_prices.csv — your full dataset budget_items.csv — only items cheaper than your chosen threshold python · your turn import pandas as pd my_data = { "item": ["bread", "milk", "eggs", "cheese", "rice", "tomatoes"], "price": [1.29, 0.99, 2.49, 3.79, 0.89, 1.49], "category": ["bakery", "dairy", "dairy", "dairy", "grains", "vegetables"] } df = pd.dataframe(my_data) # save your full dataset df.to_csv("my_prices.csv", index=false) # read it back in loaded = pd.read_csv("my_prices.csv") # save a filtered version budget = loaded[loaded["price"] run clear congratulations — you have completed book 4. you can now load, inspect, clean, filter, aggregate, sort, enrich, save, and reload tabular data using pandas. these are the core skills of practical data analysis. the next book covers web scraping: how to collect data automatically from websites using python.

chapter: Chapter One — What is Web Scraping?
partTitle: The Web as a Data Source
text: part one the web as a data source n ews websites, public institutions, and company sites publish data every day, but they publish it as webpages for humans to read. a page of latest news already contains structured information: article titles, links, dates, authors, and categories. the job of a scraper is to read that structure automatically. in this book, the live source is the latest-news page of kathimerini. by the end of chapter 8, you will have a script that visits the first page, then page 2, page 3, and so on, extracts the article titles and links, and stores everything in csv format. what you need: two python libraries — requests to fetch the page and beautifulsoup4 to read the html. install them once from the terminal with pip install requests beautifulsoup4 pandas , or run !pip install requests beautifulsoup4 pandas in a jupyter notebook cell. they are already loaded in this browser environment.

chapter: Chapter One — What is Web Scraping?
text: part two html is just text when your browser opens a webpage, what it receives is a text file full of html tags. a title is not a magical object. it is just text inside tags like <a> , <span> , and <time> . here is a small realistic slice of the kathimerini page as raw html: python · try it html = """ 22.04.2026 • 09:42 στο υπουργείο δικαιοσύνης η κοβέσι – συνάντηση με φλωρίδη σοφία σπίγγου """ print(html) run clear the browser turns that text into a nicely formatted news card. python sees the same thing as a string, which is exactly what makes it machine-readable. tags and attributes: the visible text is inside the tags. extra information such as the article url or publication timestamp lives in the attributes: href="..." and datetime="..." .

chapter: Chapter One — What is Web Scraping?
text: part three beautifulsoup to the rescue reading html with raw string slicing is fragile. beautifulsoup solves this by turning the html string into a navigable python object that you can search. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ επεκτείνεται η cordia με εξαγορά της ελληνικής ενεργειακής zeb """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") link_tag = soup.find("a") title_tag = soup.find("span") print("link tag :", link_tag.name) print("title :", title_tag.get_text(strip=true)) print("url :", link_tag.get("href")) run clear that is the core idea of scraping: find the tag, get the text, get the attribute value. later chapters will repeat this pattern with loops, dictionaries, and functions. the line soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") takes two arguments. the first is the html string you want to parse — in your scraper this will be response.text . the second is the parser : the engine that reads the raw text and builds the internal tree structure. beautifulsoup supports several parsers. the one you pass determines how the html is interpreted and how tolerant the parser is of malformed markup. parser how to use install notes html.parser beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") built into python — no install needed good default. handles real-world pages well. lxml beautifulsoup(html, "lxml") pip install lxml very fast. best choice for large pages or speed-critical scripts. lxml-xml beautifulsoup(xml, "lxml-xml") pip install lxml for xml documents, not html. html5lib beautifulsoup(html, "html5lib") pip install html5lib parses exactly like a browser. slowest, but most lenient with broken html. note on load time: the first time you press run in a book 5 chapter, the page loads python, beautifulsoup, and pandas. this can take a few extra seconds. after that, cells run instantly.

chapter: Chapter One — What is Web Scraping?
partTitle: What You Can and Cannot Scrape
text: part four what you can and cannot scrape before scraping a site, check whether there is an api or a direct download first. if not, look at the site's robots.txt file and be polite with your request rate. a latest-news page is a classic beginner use case: public, regularly updated, and clearly structured. you still have to behave responsibly and avoid making unnecessary requests. every well-maintained site publishes a robots.txt file at its root — for example https://www.kathimerini.gr/robots.txt . it tells crawlers which paths they may and may not visit. here is a simplified example: user-agent: * allow: /epikairothta/ allow: /epikairothta/page/ disallow: /wp-admin/ crawl-delay: 10 user-agent: * means the rules apply to all bots. allow lines list paths that are explicitly permitted — scraping /epikairothta/ and its paginated sub-pages is fine. disallow: /wp-admin/ means the admin backend is off-limits. crawl-delay: 10 asks crawlers to wait at least 10 seconds between requests — a rule you should respect when running a script that fetches many pages in a row. the short checklist: is there an api? is there a csv download? does robots.txt allow the path? are you using delays and small page counts while learning? if yes, you are on the right track.

chapter: Chapter One — What is Web Scraping?
text: part five your turn — read the structure prettify() re-formats html so you can inspect its structure before writing selectors. this is one of the best debugging habits in scraping. python · your turn from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ επεκτείνεται η cordia με εξαγορά της ελληνικής ενεργειακής zeb στο υπουργείο δικαιοσύνης η κοβέσι – συνάντηση με φλωρίδη """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") links = soup.find_all("a", class_="mainlink") print("article links found:", len(links)) print() print(soup.prettify()) run clear what you learned in this chapter: that web pages are structured text; that article titles and links live in predictable tags and attributes; that beautifulsoup turns raw html into a searchable object; and that prettify() helps you read the structure before you scrape it. in the next chapter you will fetch the real kathimerini page with requests .

chapter: Chapter Two — Getting HTML with requests
partTitle: Two Lines to Fetch a Page
text: part one two lines to fetch a page t he first line makes the http request. the second reads the html text that came back from the server. python · copy to your notebook import requests url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/" response = requests.get(url) html = response.text print("status code:", response.status_code) print("characters received:", len(html)) print("first 200 characters:") print(html[:200]) copy response.text is the raw html string. response.status_code tells you whether the request succeeded: 200 means success, 404 means the page was not found, and 403 means the server refused the request. why these cells do not run here. the browser version of the course cannot make live requests to external sites. copy these cells into a jupyter notebook and run them there.

chapter: Chapter Two — Getting HTML with requests
text: part two looking like a browser real sites often expect browser-like headers. the most important one is user-agent , which tells the server what browser you are using. for greek content, it also helps to set accept-language . without these, many sites return a 403 forbidden or serve you a stripped-down page. where do the right headers come from? the safest source is your own browser. when you visit a page, your browser sends a complete set of headers to the server. you can see them by opening the devtools network tab . here is how: open the page you want to scrape in chrome or firefox (e.g. https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/ ). press f12 (windows / linux) or cmd ⌘ + option ⌥ + i (mac) to open devtools. click the network tab at the top of the devtools panel. reload the page with cmd ⌘ + r (mac) or ctrl + r (windows). you will see a list of requests appear. click the very first request in the list — it is the one for the page itself, not for images or scripts. its name matches the url you typed. in the panel that opens on the right, scroll down to the request headers section. you will see a list like this: example — what devtools shows you: accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,... accept-encoding: gzip, deflate, br accept-language: el-gr,el;q=0.9,en-us;q=0.8 cache-control: max-age=0 user-agent: mozilla/5.0 (macintosh; intel mac os x 10_15_7) applewebkit/537.36 (khtml, like gecko) chrome/124.0.0.0 safari/537.36 right-click on any header line and choose copy value , or select all the text and copy it. you now have the raw header list your real browser sent. ask claude to format the headers for you the headers in devtools are shown as plain text, one per line. you need them as a python dictionary. the easiest way is to paste the raw text into claude and ask: prompt you can copy to claude: here are the request headers from chrome devtools. please convert them into a python dictionary called headers that i can pass to requests.get() . format the result as clean python code. [paste your headers here] claude will return something like this, ready to paste straight into your notebook: python · copy to your notebook import requests url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/" headers = { "user-agent": ( "mozilla/5.0 (macintosh; intel mac os x 10_15_7) " "applewebkit/537.36 (khtml, like gecko) " "chrome/124.0.0.0 safari/537.36" ), "accept-language": "el-gr,el;q=0.9,en-us;q=0.8", "accept": "text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8", } response = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=30) print("status :", response.status_code) print("encoding :", response.encoding) copy always set a timeout. without timeout=30 , your code can wait forever on a slow server. basic tools from earlier books are already in play: variables store the url and headers, the headers are a dictionary, and function calls like requests.get(...) return values that you save into variables.

chapter: Chapter Two — Getting HTML with requests
text: part three from response to soup once you have the html string, the next step is always the same: hand it to beautifulsoup. python · copy to your notebook import requests from bs4 import beautifulsoup url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/" headers = { "user-agent": ( "mozilla/5.0 (macintosh; intel mac os x 10_15_7) " "applewebkit/537.36 (khtml, like gecko) " "chrome/124.0.0.0 safari/537.36" ), "accept-language": "el-gr,el;q=0.9,en-us;q=0.8", } response = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=30) soup = beautifulsoup(response.text, "html.parser") print("page title:", soup.title.get_text(strip=true)) first_card = soup.find("a", class_="mainlink") first_title = first_card.find("span", class_="card-title") print("first article:", first_title.get_text(" ", strip=true)) copy from this point on, the requests part stays mostly the same. the interesting work is what you do with soup . here are the main methods you will use throughout this book: what you want method example first tag that matches find() soup.find("a") all tags that match find_all() soup.find_all("a") tag by class name find(class_=) soup.find("a", class_="mainlink") tag by id find(id=) soup.find(id="main") visible text inside a tag get_text() tag.get_text(" ", strip=true) value of an attribute get() tag.get("href") tags matching a css selector select() soup.select("a.mainlink") current page structure: as of april 22, 2026, the kathimerini latest-news page exposes article links with <a class="mainlink"> and titles inside <span class="card-title"> .

chapter: Chapter Two — Getting HTML with requests
text: part four moving from page 1 to page 2 the first page is https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/ . the second page is https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/page/2/ . this pattern will later let you loop through many pages with range() . python · copy to your notebook import requests from bs4 import beautifulsoup base_url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/" headers = { "user-agent": ( "mozilla/5.0 (macintosh; intel mac os x 10_15_7) " "applewebkit/537.36 (khtml, like gecko) " "chrome/124.0.0.0 safari/537.36" ), "accept-language": "el-gr,el;q=0.9,en-us;q=0.8", } page_number = 2 page_url = f"{base_url}page/{page_number}/" response = requests.get(page_url, headers=headers, timeout=30) soup = beautifulsoup(response.text, "html.parser") print("fetched:", page_url) print("status :", response.status_code) print("cards :", len(soup.find_all("a", class_="mainlink"))) copy this is the first important pagination idea of the book: some pages are not discovered by clicking buttons in python. you build the next url as a string. special case for page 1: page 1 uses the base url, but later pages use page/2/ , page/3/ , and so on. in the final chapter you will place that if/else logic directly inside a for loop over range() .

chapter: Chapter Two — Getting HTML with requests
text: part five your turn — fetch and explore copy this block into your notebook. it fetches the live page and prints the first five titles it finds. python · copy to your notebook import requests from bs4 import beautifulsoup url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/" headers = { "user-agent": ( "mozilla/5.0 (macintosh; intel mac os x 10_15_7) " "applewebkit/537.36 (khtml, like gecko) " "chrome/124.0.0.0 safari/537.36" ), "accept-language": "el-gr,el;q=0.9,en-us;q=0.8", } response = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=30) soup = beautifulsoup(response.text, "html.parser") cards = soup.find_all("a", class_="mainlink") print("total article links found:", len(cards)) print() for card in cards[:5]: title_tag = card.find("span", class_="card-title") if not title_tag: continue title = title_tag.get_text(" ", strip=true) link = card.get("href") print(title) print(link) print() copy what you learned in this chapter: how requests.get() fetches a live page; what response.text and response.status_code contain; how to send browser-like headers; how to turn the html into soup ; and how page 2 follows the url pattern .../page/2/ . in the next chapters you will use beautifulsoup to extract the exact fields you want.

chapter: Chapter Three — Your First BeautifulSoup Object
text: part one creating a soup object y ou create a beautifulsoup object by passing two things: the html string and a parser name . the parser is the engine that reads the raw text and builds the internal structure. use "html.parser" — it is built into python and works for every real-world page you will encounter in this course. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ δασικές πυρκαγιές 2 active incidents in attica. """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # soup is not a string — it is a special beautifulsoup object print("type:", type(soup)) print() # prettify() re-formats the html with clean indentation print(soup.prettify()) run clear the prettify() output is not the actual data — it is a debugging view. you can see every tag, every attribute, every level of nesting. whenever you are unsure what a page contains, run print(soup.prettify()) first and read the structure before writing any selectors. note on load time: the first time you press run in a book 5 chapter, the page loads both python and the beautifulsoup library. this can take 5–15 seconds. after that, all cells run instantly.

chapter: Chapter Three — Your First BeautifulSoup Object
text:
part two accessing tags by name the simplest way to reach an element is to access it as a property of the soup object — just type soup. followed by the tag name. soup.h3 gives you the first

on the page. soup.p gives you the first

. soup.table gives you the first . python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ δασικές πυρκαγιές αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου έναρξη: 12/07/2024 """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # access the h3 tag directly heading = soup.h3 print("h3 tag:", heading) print() # access the first div first_div = soup.div print("first div:", first_div.name) print("its class:", first_div.get("class")) run clear notice that soup.div gives you the outermost
— the tabcontent one — not the inner panel div. tag-name access always returns the first match in document order. to reach specific elements deeper in the page you will use find() and find_all() , which you will learn in the next chapter. tags have a .name property: every beautifulsoup tag object knows its own name: soup.h3.name returns the string "h3" . this is useful when you are iterating over mixed elements and need to check what type each one is.

chapter: Chapter Three — Your First BeautifulSoup Object
text:
part three reading text with get_text() every beautifulsoup tag has a get_text() method. it strips away all the html tags inside the element and returns only the human-readable text. this is the main method you will use throughout the book to extract actual values from elements. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου τύπος: δάσος κωνοφόρων σε εξέλιξη """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") heading = soup.find("div", class_="panel-heading") # get_text() with no arguments — all text joined together print("no separator:") print(repr(heading.get_text())) print() # get_text(" ", strip=true) — joined with spaces, whitespace trimmed print("with separator and strip:") print(heading.get_text(" ", strip=true)) run clear the difference matters. without arguments, get_text() runs all the text together with only the original whitespace — which can produce strings like "
 αττική
 τύπος: ...
" . passing " " as a separator and strip=true gives you a clean, readable string. always use get_text(" ", strip=true) unless you specifically need the raw form. get_text() vs .string: a tag also has a .string attribute that returns the text only if the element contains no child tags — just plain text. if there are child tags, .string returns none . use get_text() because it always works, regardless of inner structure.

chapter: Chapter Three — Your First BeautifulSoup Object
text: part four navigating into nested tags you can chain tag-name access to navigate down into the document tree. soup.div.table.tr.td walks down the nesting — outermost div, then the table inside it, then the row inside the table, then the first cell. each step returns the first matching child. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου έναρξη: 12/07/2024 """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # navigate step by step outer_div = soup.div inner_div = outer_div.div table = inner_div.table row = table.tr first_cell = row.td print("outer div class:", outer_div.get("class")) print("inner div class:", inner_div.get("class")) print("first cell text:", first_cell.get_text(strip=true)) # or chain it all in one line print() print("chained:", soup.div.div.table.tr.td.get_text(strip=true)) run clear chaining is convenient for simple, predictable structures. in practice, real pages have more variation — sometimes a tag is missing, sometimes there are extra wrappers. the find() method you will learn next handles these cases more robustly, so it is what you will use most often. none propagates: if any step in the chain finds nothing (because the tag does not exist), it returns none . the next .something on none will raise an attributeerror . the safe version of the chain above is soup.find("td") , which you will learn in the next chapter.

chapter: Chapter Three — Your First BeautifulSoup Object
partTitle: Your Turn — Read a Card's Contents
