text: part five your turn — read a card's contents the cell below contains a complete incident card. use soup. tag-name access and get_text() to extract three things: the section heading, the location from the first cell, and the start date from the second cell. python · your turn from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ δασικές πυρκαγιές (1) αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου έναρξη: 12/07/2024 """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # 1. get the section heading text heading_text = soup.h3.get_text(strip=true) print("section:", heading_text) # 2. get the first table cell (location) location = soup.td.get_text(strip=true) print("location:", location) # 3. get all table cells and read the second one (start date) all_cells = soup.find_all("td") start_date = all_cells[1].get_text(strip=true) print("start:", start_date) run clear what you learned in this chapter: how to create a beautifulsoup object with beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") ; how prettify() helps you inspect the structure; how to access tags directly with dot notation; how get_text(" ", strip=true) extracts clean text; and how to chain tag access to navigate nested structures. in the next chapter you will use find() and find_all() to search for elements across the whole document.

chapter: Chapter Four — find() and find_all()
text: part one find() — the first match find() searches the document and returns the first element that matches. pass a tag name and beautifulsoup returns the first tag with that name. if nothing matches, it returns none . python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ δασικές πυρκαγιές αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου έναρξη: 12/07/2024 στερεά ελλάδα - δήμος θήβας έναρξη: 10/07/2024 """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # find() returns the first match heading = soup.find("h3") print("heading:", heading.get_text(strip=true)) first_td = soup.find("td") print("first cell:", first_td.get_text(strip=true)) # when nothing matches, find() returns none missing = soup.find("h1") print("h1 found:", missing) run clear always check for none before calling methods on a find() result. if you call .get_text() on none , python raises an attributeerror that crashes your script. the safe pattern is if heading: print(heading.get_text()) . find() vs dot notation: soup.h3 and soup.find("h3") do the same thing — both return the first <h3> . use dot notation for quick exploration. use find() in real scraping code because it is more flexible: you can pass a tag name, a class, an id, or any attribute.

chapter: Chapter Four — find() and find_all()
text: part two find_all() — every match find_all() returns a list of every element that matches. even if there is only one match you get a list with one item. if there are no matches you get an empty list — never none . this predictability makes it easy to loop over results. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου έναρξη: 12/07/2024 αττική - δήμος λαυρεωτικής έναρξη: 14/07/2024 στερεά ελλάδα - δήμος θήβας έναρξη: 10/07/2024 """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # find_all() returns a list all_divs = soup.find_all("div") print("total divs:", len(all_divs)) all_cells = soup.find_all("td") print("total cells:", len(all_cells)) # loop over all cells print() print("all cell contents:") for cell in all_cells: print(" ", cell.get_text(strip=true)) run clear notice that find_all("div") returns 4 divs — the outer tabcontent plus the three panels. find_all() searches the entire document tree, including nested elements. in the next chapter you will learn how to filter by class name to get only the panels you want. find vs find_all: use find() when you expect exactly one result — the page title, a unique badge. use find_all() when you are collecting a list of things — all incident cards, all table rows. when find_all() returns an empty list when you expected results, the page layout has changed.

chapter: Chapter Four — find() and find_all()
text: part three searching inside a tag you can call find() and find_all() on any tag , not just the root soup object. calling them on a tag limits the search to that tag's contents. this is the key pattern for structured data: first find the container, then search inside it. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου έναρξη: 12/07/2024 στερεά ελλάδα - δήμος θήβας έναρξη: 10/07/2024 """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # get all panels (we'll learn to filter by class next chapter) all_panels = soup.find_all("div") # the first div is the tabcontent wrapper, panels start at index 1 panels = all_panels[1:] # skip the first outer div for panel in panels: # search for cells inside this panel only cells = panel.find_all("td") location = cells[0].get_text(strip=true) start = cells[1].get_text(strip=true) print(f"location: {location}") print(f"start: {start}") print() run clear by calling panel.find_all("td") instead of soup.find_all("td") , you only get the cells inside that specific panel — not cells from other panels. this scoped search is essential whenever you want to keep related data together. in the next chapter you will also learn how to find panels by their class name, which is cleaner than using list indices. the core scraping pattern: find_all() to get a list of containers → loop over the list → find() or find_all() inside each container to get its contents. this pattern repeats in almost every scraper ever written.

chapter: Chapter Four — find() and find_all()
text: part four searching for multiple tag types sometimes the element you want might be tagged as <h3> on one page and <h4> on another — sites are not always consistent. you can pass a list of tag names to find() or find_all() and it will match any of them: python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup # two pages with different heading levels for the category name html_v1 = """ δασικές πυρκαγιές (2) incident a """ html_v2 = """ δασικές πυρκαγιές (1) incident b """ for html in [html_v1, html_v2]: soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # pass a list — matches h3 or h4, whichever exists heading = soup.find(["h3", "h4"]) print("heading found:", heading.get_text(strip=true)) run clear this technique is especially useful for scrapers that need to stay working as a site evolves. if a site changes a heading from <h3> to <h4> , code that uses find(["h3", "h4"]) keeps working without any extra changes. defensive scraping: real pages change. writing find(["h3", "h4"]) instead of find("h3") costs nothing and makes your scraper resilient to minor layout changes. any time you know two variations exist, handle both.

chapter: Chapter Four — find() and find_all()
text: part five your turn — count and loop the page below has multiple sections, each with a heading and several panels. use find_all() to count all the panels, then loop over them and print each one's text content. python · your turn from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ δασικές πυρκαγιές (2) αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου έναρξη: 12/07/2024 στερεά ελλάδα - δήμος θήβας έναρξη: 10/07/2024 αγροτοδασικές πυρκαγιές (1) πελοπόννησος - δήμος σπάρτης έναρξη: 15/07/2024 """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # how many sections are there? sections = soup.find_all("div", {"class": "tabcontent"}) print("sections:", len(sections)) # how many panels total? all_panels = soup.find_all("div", {"class": "panel"}) print("panels total:", len(all_panels)) print() # loop over each section and print its heading + panel count for section in sections: heading = section.find(["h3", "h4"]) panels = section.find_all("div") print("section:", heading.get_text(strip=true)) print("panels in section:", len(panels)) print() run clear what you learned in this chapter: find() returns the first match or none ; find_all() returns a list of all matches; you can call either on any tag to search only inside it; and passing a list of tag names lets you match multiple alternatives. in the next chapter you will add one more powerful tool: searching by css class name, which lets you target exactly the elements you want.

chapter: Chapter Five — Searching by Class and id
text: part one searching by class name t he class_ argument (note the underscore — python's built-in class keyword would conflict without it) lets you filter by css class name. pass it to find() or find_all() alongside a tag name and you get only the elements that have that class: python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου αττική - δήμος λαυρεωτικής στερεά ελλάδα - δήμος θήβας """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # all panels (any class containing "panel") all_panels = soup.find_all("div", class_="panel") print("all panels:", len(all_panels)) # only active (red) panels active = soup.find_all("div", class_="panel-red") print("active:", len(active)) # only contained (green) panels contained = soup.find_all("div", class_="panel-green") print("contained:", len(contained)) run clear an element can have multiple class names at once — class="panel panel-red" means the element belongs to both the panel group and the panel-red group. beautifulsoup's class_ argument matches if the specified name is anywhere in the class list, so class_="panel-red" correctly finds elements whose class attribute is "panel panel-red" . class_ vs class: python uses class as a keyword for defining new classes. beautifulsoup uses class_ (with a trailing underscore) as the argument name to avoid the conflict. do not forget the underscore — class= will raise a syntaxerror .

chapter: Chapter Five — Searching by Class and id
partTitle: Reading Status from the Class Name
text: part two reading status from the class name the class name is data, not just a styling hook. when an incident card uses panel-red for active fires and panel-green for contained ones, the class name itself tells you the status. you can read it with the .get("class") method, which returns the class list as a python list of strings: python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου στερεά ελλάδα - δήμος θήβας πελοπόννησος - δήμος σπάρτης """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") status_labels = { "panel-red": "σε εξέλιξη", "panel-green": "πλήρως ελεγχόμενη", "panel-yellow": "μερικώς ελεγχόμενη", } for panel in soup.find_all("div", class_="panel"): class_list = panel.get("class") # e.g. ["panel", "panel-red"] location = panel.find("td").get_text(strip=true) # find which status class is present status = "" for css_class in class_list: if css_class in status_labels: status = status_labels[css_class] print(f"{location} → {status}") run clear .get("class") returns a list like ["panel", "panel-red"] . the loop checks each class name in the list against the status dictionary and sets the status when it finds a match. this is cleaner than hardcoding "panel-red" in every comparison. .get() vs ["class"]: you can access an attribute with tag["class"] (like a dictionary) or with tag.get("class") . the difference: tag["class"] raises a keyerror if the attribute is missing; tag.get("class") returns none instead. use .get() for any attribute that might be absent.

chapter: Chapter Five — Searching by Class and id
text: part three searching by id the id attribute uniquely identifies one element on a page. when you know the id, you can jump directly to that element without searching the whole tree. pass id="the-id" to find() : python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ δασικές πυρκαγιές (2) αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου στερεά ελλάδα αστικές πυρκαγιές (1) αθήνα - κολωνάκι """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # jump directly to the forest fires section by id forest_section = soup.find(id="forest-fires") heading = forest_section.find("h3") panels = forest_section.find_all("div", class_="panel") print("section:", heading.get_text(strip=true)) print("panels:", len(panels)) print() for panel in panels: print(" ", panel.find("td").get_text(strip=true)) run clear once you have the section by id, every subsequent search is scoped to that section. calling forest_section.find_all("div", class_="panel") returns only the panels inside the forest fires section, not the panel in the urban fires section. id is unique — class is a group: only one element per page should have a given id. any number of elements can share the same class name. use id= when you need to pinpoint one specific element; use class_= when you want to collect a category of elements.

chapter: Chapter Five — Searching by Class and id
text: part four css selectors with select() beautifulsoup also supports css selector syntax through the select() method. if you have ever written css stylesheets, you already know these patterns. select() always returns a list; select_one() returns the first match or none . soup.select("div") — all <div> elements soup.select(".panel-red") — elements with class panel-red soup.select("#forest-fires") — the element with id forest-fires soup.select("div.panel td") — <td> elements inside a div.panel python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ δασικές πυρκαγιές αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου έναρξη: 12/07/2024 στερεά ελλάδα - δήμος θήβας έναρξη: 10/07/2024 """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # all location cells using a css selector locations = soup.select("td.location") print("locations:") for td in locations: print(" ", td.get_text(strip=true)) # only timing cells inside red panels red_timing = soup.select("div.panel-red td.timing") print() print("timing of active fires:") for td in red_timing: print(" ", td.get_text(strip=true)) run clear select() or find_all() — which to use? they produce the same results. use select() when the css selector syntax is more concise — especially when you need to combine tag and class in one expression like "div.panel-red td" . use find_all() when you prefer explicit keyword arguments. both are correct.

chapter: Chapter Five — Searching by Class and id
text: part five your turn — active fires only use find_all() with class_="panel-red" to extract only the active incidents from the page below. for each active panel, print the location and start date. python · your turn from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ δασικές πυρκαγιές (3) αττική - δήμος μαρκόπουλου έναρξη: 12/07/2024 στερεά ελλάδα - δήμος θήβας έναρξη: 10/07/2024 αττική - δήμος λαυρεωτικής έναρξη: 14/07/2024 """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # get only the active (red) panels active_panels = soup.find_all("div", class_="panel-red") print(f"active fires: {len(active_panels)}") print() for panel in active_panels: cells = panel.find_all("td") location = cells[0].get_text(strip=true) start = cells[1].get_text(strip=true) print(f" location : {location}") print(f" start : {start}") print() run clear what you learned in this chapter: how to use class_= to filter elements by css class name; how to read the class list from a tag with .get("class") ; how to jump to a unique element with find(id="...") ; and how select() lets you use css selector syntax for concise targeting. in the next chapter you will go deeper into extracting text and attributes — including link urls and custom data attributes.

chapter: Chapter Six — Extracting Text and Attributes
text: part one stripped_strings — line by line w hen a tag contains nested text on separate lines, stripped_strings gives you the clean pieces one by one. this is useful when a card contains the date, title, and author as separate text nodes. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ 22.04.2026 • 09:42 στο υπουργείο δικαιοσύνης η κοβέσι συνάντηση με φλωρίδη σοφία σπίγγου """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") article = soup.find("div", class_="article-item") lines = list(article.stripped_strings) print("all text lines:") for line in lines: print(" ", line) run clear this gives you separate text nodes instead of one long messy string. you will often use it together with get_text(" ", strip=true) when a title contains line breaks. good default: first inspect list(tag.stripped_strings) . if the text is already nicely separated, keep it. if you want one clean sentence, use tag.get_text(" ", strip=true) .

chapter: Chapter Six — Extracting Text and Attributes
text: part two reading attributes the visible title is text. the article url and publication timestamp are attributes. use tag.get("href") and tag.get("datetime") to read them. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ 22.04.2026 • 09:42 στο υπουργείο δικαιοσύνης η κοβέσι – συνάντηση με φλωρίδη """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") time_tag = soup.find("time") link_tag = soup.find("a", class_="mainlink") title_tag = soup.find("span", class_="card-title") print("datetime:", time_tag.get("datetime")) print("title :", title_tag.get_text(" ", strip=true)) print("href :", link_tag.get("href")) run clear attributes often hold the most useful machine-readable values on a page. the title is for humans. the link and the iso datetime are perfect for data collection. use .get() for safety: tag["href"] crashes if the attribute is missing. tag.get("href") returns none instead.

chapter: Chapter Six — Extracting Text and Attributes
text: part three navigating with .parent sometimes you find the smallest useful tag first, like span.card-title , and then need to move upward to reach the link or the full article card. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ επεκτείνεται η cordia με εξαγορά της ελληνικής ενεργειακής zeb """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") title_tag = soup.find("span", class_="card-title") link_tag = title_tag.parent article_tag = link_tag.parent print("current tag :", title_tag.name) print("parent link :", link_tag.name, link_tag.get("class")) print("article card:", article_tag.name, article_tag.get("class")) print("href :", link_tag.get("href")) run clear this pattern is common in scraping: locate the most specific tag first, then climb to the surrounding container. think in containers: once you have the full article card, you can search inside it for the time, title, and author without mixing data from nearby cards.

chapter: Chapter Six — Extracting Text and Attributes
text: part four find_next_sibling() the article card places related fields next to each other: time, then link, then author. find_next_sibling() lets you move across that structure. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ 22.04.2026 • 09:42 στο υπουργείο δικαιοσύνης η κοβέσι – συνάντηση με φλωρίδη σοφία σπίγγου """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") posted_on = soup.find("span", class_="posted-on") link_tag = posted_on.find_next_sibling("a") author_tag = link_tag.find_next_sibling("span") print("time :", posted_on.get_text(" ", strip=true)) print("title :", link_tag.get_text(" ", strip=true)) print("author:", author_tag.get_text(" ", strip=true)) run clear use find_next_sibling() , not .next_sibling . the raw sibling is often just a newline or whitespace node. good use case: when a page puts fields side-by-side instead of nesting them inside the same tag, sibling navigation is usually the cleanest solution.

chapter: Chapter Six — Extracting Text and Attributes
text: part five your turn — build one record extract the date, title, author, and link from the card below, then store them in a dictionary. python · your turn from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ 22.04.2026 • 08:53 πού βγαίνουμε σήμερα τετάρτη; προτάσεις για θέατρο, συναυλίες, εκθέσεις, εκδηλώσεις ελένη σαμπάνη """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") article = soup.find("div", class_="article-item") time_tag = article.find("time") link_tag = article.find("a", class_="mainlink") title_tag = article.find("span", class_="card-title") author_tag = article.find("span", class_="author-link") record = { "published_at": time_tag.get("datetime"), "title": title_tag.get_text(" ", strip=true), "author": author_tag.get_text(" ", strip=true), "link": link_tag.get("href"), } print(record) run clear what you learned in this chapter: how to extract text cleanly, how to read attributes like href and datetime , how to move upward with .parent , how to move sideways with find_next_sibling() , and how to turn one article card into one python dictionary. in the next chapter you will do this for many cards and load the result into a dataframe.

chapter: Chapter Seven — From Dictionaries to DataFrames
text: part one one card becomes one dictionary t he natural unit of scraped data is a dictionary. one article card becomes one dictionary with named fields like title , link , author , and published_at . python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ 22.04.2026 • 09:53 επεκτείνεται η cordia με εξαγορά της ελληνικής ενεργειακής zeb ηλίας μπέλλος """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") article = soup.find("div", class_="article-item") record = { "published_at": article.find("time").get("datetime"), "title": article.find("span", class_="card-title").get_text(" ", strip=true), "author": article.find("span", class_="author-link").get_text(" ", strip=true), "link": article.find("a", class_="mainlink").get("href"), } print(record) run clear this uses basic python you already know: strings, variables, dictionaries, and function calls. scraping is not a new language. it is ordinary python applied to html.

chapter: Chapter Seven — From Dictionaries to DataFrames
partTitle: Many Cards Become a List of Dicts
text: part two many cards become a list of dicts the next step is a loop: find all article cards, create one dictionary per card, and append() each dictionary to a list. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ επεκτείνεται η cordia με εξαγορά της ελληνικής ενεργειακής zeb στο υπουργείο δικαιοσύνης η κοβέσι – συνάντηση με φλωρίδη πού βγαίνουμε σήμερα τετάρτη; προτάσεις για θέατρο, συναυλίες, εκθέσεις, εκδηλώσεις """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") cards = soup.find_all("a", class_="mainlink") records = [] for card in cards: title_tag = card.find("span", class_="card-title") if not title_tag: continue record = { "title": title_tag.get_text(" ", strip=true), "link": card.get("href"), } records.append(record) print("records:", len(records)) for record in records: print(record) run clear this is the core data-collection loop of the whole book: find many containers, loop over them, build dictionaries, append them to a list. books 1, 2, and 3 all appear here: variables, list creation, for loops, if guards, dictionaries, and append() . the scraper works because these basic tools combine well.

chapter: Chapter Seven — From Dictionaries to DataFrames
text: part three list of dicts to dataframe pandas reads a list of dictionaries directly. each dictionary becomes one row. each key becomes one column. python · try it import pandas as pd records = [ { "title": "επεκτείνεται η cordia με εξαγορά της ελληνικής ενεργειακής zeb", "link": "/economy/business/564187087/epekteinetai-i-cordia-me-exagora-tis-ellinikis-energeiakis-zeb/", }, { "title": "στο υπουργείο δικαιοσύνης η κοβέσι – συνάντηση με φλωρίδη", "link": "/society/dikastiko/564187411/sto-ypoyrgeio-dikaiosynis-i-kovesi-synantisi-me-floridi/", }, ] df = pd.dataframe(records) print(df.to_string(index=false)) print() print("rows :", len(df)) print("columns:", list(df.columns)) run clear why dataframes matter: once the data is in pandas, filtering, sorting, exporting, and later analysis become much easier.

chapter: Chapter Seven — From Dictionaries to DataFrames
text: part four saving the first csv a dataframe can be written to disk with to_csv() . this is the point where scraping becomes useful outside the notebook. python · copy to your notebook import requests from bs4 import beautifulsoup import pandas as pd url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/" base_url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr" headers = { "user-agent": ( "mozilla/5.0 (macintosh; intel mac os x 10_15_7) " "applewebkit/537.36 (khtml, like gecko) " "chrome/124.0.0.0 safari/537.36" ), "accept-language": "el-gr,el;q=0.9,en-us;q=0.8", } response = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=30) soup = beautifulsoup(response.text, "html.parser") records = [] for card in soup.find_all("a", class_="mainlink"): title_tag = card.find("span", class_="card-title") if not title_tag: continue href = card.get("href", "") if href.startswith("/"): href = base_url + href records.append({ "title": title_tag.get_text(" ", strip=true), "link": href, }) df = pd.dataframe(records) df.to_csv("kathimerini_page_1.csv", index=false) print("saved:", "kathimerini_page_1.csv") print("rows :", len(df)) copy the csv file will contain one row per article and two columns: title and link. make links absolute: many sites use relative links like /society/... . add the base url before saving so the csv contains complete links that work everywhere.

chapter: Chapter Seven — From Dictionaries to DataFrames
text: part five your turn — scrape one page the snapshot below contains four article cards. extract the titles and links, build a dataframe, and preview the csv text. python · your turn from bs4 import beautifulsoup import pandas as pd html = """ επεκτείνεται η cordia με εξαγορά της ελληνικής ενεργειακής zeb στο υπουργείο δικαιοσύνης η κοβέσι – συνάντηση με φλωρίδη σούπερ λιγκ 1: κρίσιμη αγωνιστική για τα πλέι άουτ πού βγαίνουμε σήμερα τετάρτη; προτάσεις για θέατρο, συναυλίες, εκθέσεις, εκδηλώσεις """ base_url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr" soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") records = [] for card in soup.find_all("a", class_="mainlink"): title = card.find("span", class_="card-title").get_text(" ", strip=true) link = base_url + card.get("href") records.append({"title": title, "link": link}) df = pd.dataframe(records) print(df.to_string(index=false)) print() print(df.to_csv(index=false)) run clear what you learned in this chapter: how one article becomes one dictionary, how many dictionaries become a list, how pandas turns that list into a dataframe, and how to save a one-page scraper to csv. in the final chapter you will repeat the same idea across page 1, page 2, page 3, and beyond.

chapter: Chapter Eight — Pagination and the Full Pipeline
text: part one cleaning titles and links r aw text and raw urls often need one small cleaning step before you store them. titles may contain extra whitespace. links may be relative paths that need the site's base url. python · try it base_url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr" raw_title = """ στο υπουργείο δικαιοσύνης η κοβέσι – συνάντηση με φλωρίδη """ raw_href = "/society/dikastiko/564187411/sto-ypoyrgeio-dikaiosynis-i-kovesi-synantisi-me-floridi/" clean_title = " ".join(raw_title.split()) if raw_href.startswith("/"): clean_link = base_url + raw_href else: clean_link = raw_href print("title:", clean_title) print("link :", clean_link) run clear two useful habits: clean the text before storing it, and save full absolute links in your csv whenever possible.

chapter: Chapter Eight — Pagination and the Full Pipeline
text: part two building the logic inline you do not need extra abstractions to scrape multiple pages. you can build the page url, clean the link, and create the article dictionaries directly inside the loop. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup base_url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr" start_url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/" html = """ επεκτείνεται η cordia με εξαγορά της ελληνικής ενεργειακής zeb στο υπουργείο δικαιοσύνης η κοβέσι – συνάντηση με φλωρίδη """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") page_number = 2 if page_number == 1: page_url = start_url else: page_url = f"{start_url}page/{page_number}/" records = [] for position, card in enumerate(soup.find_all("a", class_="mainlink"), start=1): title_tag = card.find("span", class_="card-title") if not title_tag: continue href = card.get("href", "") if href.startswith("/"): href = base_url + href records.append({ "page": page_number, "position": position, "title": " ".join(title_tag.get_text(" ", strip=true).split()), "link": href, }) print(page_url) print() print(records) run clear this version uses only the basic tools from the earlier books: variables, if , for , enumerate() , dictionaries, and list append() . basic python review: you can build a real multi-page scraper with just loops, conditionals, dictionaries, and lists.

chapter: Chapter Eight — Pagination and the Full Pipeline
text: part three the complete one-page scraper before looping through many pages, make sure page 1 works cleanly from end to end. python · copy to your notebook import requests from bs4 import beautifulsoup import pandas as pd base_url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr" start_url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/" headers = { "user-agent": ( "mozilla/5.0 (macintosh; intel mac os x 10_15_7) " "applewebkit/537.36 (khtml, like gecko) " "chrome/124.0.0.0 safari/537.36" ), "accept-language": "el-gr,el;q=0.9,en-us;q=0.8", } response = requests.get(start_url, headers=headers, timeout=30) soup = beautifulsoup(response.text, "html.parser") records = [] for position, card in enumerate(soup.find_all("a", class_="mainlink"), start=1): title_tag = card.find("span", class_="card-title") if not title_tag: continue href = card.get("href", "") if href.startswith("/"): href = base_url + href records.append({ "page": 1, "position": position, "title": " ".join(title_tag.get_text(" ", strip=true).split()), "link": href, }) df = pd.dataframe(records) df.to_csv("kathimerini_page_1.csv", index=false) print("saved:", "kathimerini_page_1.csv") print("rows :", len(df)) copy build in stages: do not start with a multi-page loop. first make page 1 work. then generalise.

chapter: Chapter Eight — Pagination and the Full Pipeline
text: part four looping through page 1, page 2, page 3 pagination is just a loop. use range() to visit page 1, then page 2, then page 3, and collect all results into one big list. python · copy to your notebook import requests from bs4 import beautifulsoup import pandas as pd base_url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr" start_url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/" headers = { "user-agent": ( "mozilla/5.0 (macintosh; intel mac os x 10_15_7) " "applewebkit/537.36 (khtml, like gecko) " "chrome/124.0.0.0 safari/537.36" ), "accept-language": "el-gr,el;q=0.9,en-us;q=0.8", } all_records = [] for page_number in range(1, 4): if page_number == 1: page_url = start_url else: page_url = f"{start_url}page/{page_number}/" response = requests.get(page_url, headers=headers, timeout=30) soup = beautifulsoup(response.text, "html.parser") page_records = [] for position, card in enumerate(soup.find_all("a", class_="mainlink"), start=1): title_tag = card.find("span", class_="card-title") if not title_tag: continue href = card.get("href", "") if href.startswith("/"): href = base_url + href page_records.append({ "page": page_number, "position": position, "title": " ".join(title_tag.get_text(" ", strip=true).split()), "link": href, }) print(f"page {page_number}: {len(page_records)} articles") all_records.extend(page_records) df = pd.dataframe(all_records) df.to_csv("kathimerini_pages_1_to_3.csv", index=false) print() print("saved:", "kathimerini_pages_1_to_3.csv") print("total rows:", len(df)) copy that is the full pagination pattern. the special-case url logic appears directly inside the loop, and the extraction logic is written directly under it. what changed from chapter 7? only two things: a small if/else block that builds the correct page url, and a for page_number in range(...) loop that repeats the same scraper across multiple pages.

chapter: Chapter Eight — Pagination and the Full Pipeline
text: part five your turn — simulate pagination the cell below simulates page 1 and page 2 with static html, so you can run the multi-page logic directly in the browser. python · your turn from bs4 import beautifulsoup import pandas as pd base_url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr" start_url = "https://www.kathimerini.gr/epikairothta/" pages = { 1: """ επεκτείνεται η cordia με εξαγορά της ελληνικής ενεργειακής zeb στο υπουργείο δικαιοσύνης η κοβέσι – συνάντηση με φλωρίδη """, 2: """ σούπερ λιγκ 1: κρίσιμη αγωνιστική για τα πλέι άουτ πού βγαίνουμε σήμερα τετάρτη; προτάσεις για θέατρο, συναυλίες, εκθέσεις, εκδηλώσεις """, } all_records = [] for page_number in range(1, 3): if page_number == 1: page_url = start_url else: page_url = f"{start_url}page/{page_number}/" soup = beautifulsoup(pages[page_number], "html.parser") page_records = [] for position, card in enumerate(soup.find_all("a", class_="mainlink"), start=1): title_tag = card.find("span", class_="card-title") if not title_tag: continue href = card.get("href", "") if href.startswith("/"): href = base_url + href page_records.append({ "page": page_number, "position": position, "title": " ".join(title_tag.get_text(" ", strip=true).split()), "link": href, }) print(page_url, "->", len(page_records), "records") all_records.extend(page_records) df = pd.dataframe(all_records) print() print(df.to_string(index=false)) print() print(df.to_csv(index=false)) run clear what you learned in this book: how to fetch live html with requests ; how to search the page with beautifulsoup; how to extract titles and links; how to build dictionaries and dataframes; how to write csv files; and how to use loops plus range() to scrape multiple pages like .../page/2/ and .../page/3/ . you now have a complete template for scraping any paginated news feed.

chapter: Chapter Nine — Full Data Journalism Project
partTitle: Two-Level Architecture — List Page and Detail Page
text: part one two-level architecture — list page and detail page t he hellenic parliament publishes a searchable archive of daily legislative agendas. all 74 pages of that archive live under one url pattern — but unlike a news feed, each item is a link to a separate detail page. you must scrape two layers to get the data. layer 1 — the list page. each list page shows a batch of daily plans as links. the url changes only in pageno : https://www.hellenicparliament.gr/nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan?more=true&pageno=1 https://www.hellenicparliament.gr/nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan?more=true&pageno=2 … https://www.hellenicparliament.gr/nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan?more=true&pageno=74 layer 2 — the detail page. each list item carries a unique id in its href . you extract that id and build a second url: https://www.hellenicparliament.gr/nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan?lpr_id=04734c57-7b71-4e5e-86e9-b43600adcc72 the detail page contains the full text of the daily plan, including the session date. run the cell below to see how all 74 list-page urls are built with a single range() . python · try it base_url = "https://www.hellenicparliament.gr" list_path = "/nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan" total_pages = 74 for page_number in range(1, 6): url = f"{base_url}{list_path}?more=true&pageno={page_number}" print(f"page {page_number:>2}: {url}") print("...") last_url = f"{base_url}{list_path}?more=true&pageno={total_pages}" print(f"page {total_pages}: {last_url}") print() print(f"total list-page requests needed: {total_pages}") run clear two-level scraping: first collect all the ids from the list pages, then follow each id to its detail page. this pattern appears on government sites, court databases, and most institutional archives.

chapter: Chapter Nine — Full Data Journalism Project
text: part two parsing the list page each list page has a div with class pagecontent that holds a ul with one li per daily plan. each li contains one a tag. the link text is already the session date in dd/mm/yyyy format — no further parsing needed. the href carries the id you need for layer 2. the selector path is: .pagecontent → ul → li → a . python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup html = """ 24/04/2026 20/04/2026 03/04/2026 30/03/2026 20/03/2026 """ soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") container = soup.find("div", class_="pagecontent") ul = container.find("ul") items = ul.find_all("li") print(f"found {len(items)} items\n") for item in items: a = item.find("a") date = a.get_text(strip=true) href = a.get("href", "") print(f"date: {date}") print(f"href: {href}") print() run clear container.find("ul") narrows the search scope before find_all("li") runs. this keeps the code correct even if the page has other ul elements outside the content area. note on response.content : in the real scraper you pass response.content (raw bytes) to beautifulsoup instead of response.text . this lets beautifulsoup read the encoding declaration from the html <meta charset> tag and decode greek text correctly without guessing.

chapter: Chapter Nine — Full Data Journalism Project
text: part three extracting the id with regex the href from each list item looks like this: /nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan?lpr_id=04734c57-7b71-4e5e-86e9-b43600adcc72 you need only the value after lpr_id= . the pattern lpr_id=([\w-]+) captures it: [\w-] matches letters, digits, underscores, and hyphens; + means one or more. this is tighter than .* , which would consume everything including any parameters that might follow the id. python · try it import re base_url = "https://www.hellenicparliament.gr" hrefs = [ "/nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan?lpr_id=04734c57-7b71-4e5e-86e9-b43600adcc72", "/nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan?lpr_id=1a2b3c4d-5e6f-7890-abcd-ef1234567890", "/nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan?lpr_id=aaaabbbb-cccc-dddd-eeee-ffffaaaabbbb", ] for href in hrefs: match = re.search(r"lpr_id=([\w-]+)", href) if match: lpr_id = match.group(1) detail_url = f"{base_url}/nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan?lpr_id={lpr_id}" print(f"id : {lpr_id}") print(f"detail url: {detail_url}") print() run clear with the detail url built, a second requests.get() fetches that page. the full text of the daily plan lives in the same div.pagecontent container — so you reuse the same selector on the new page. match.group(1) returns the first captured group — the text inside the first pair of parentheses in your pattern. match.group(0) would return the entire matched string, including lpr_id= . why the full header set matters. the sec-fetch-dest , sec-fetch-mode , sec-fetch-site , and sec-fetch-user headers tell the server this is a real browser navigation, not a script. government sites often check for these and return an empty page or a redirect when they are missing. always copy the complete headers from your browser's network tab — stripping them down is the most common reason a scraper silently breaks.

chapter: Chapter Nine — Full Data Journalism Project
partTitle: A Bonus Skill — Parsing Greek Dates from Document Text
text:
part four a bonus skill — parsing greek dates from document text in this project the date is already available on the list page as 24/04/2026 — no extra work needed. but many greek government documents store dates in the running text of the document body, in a format like: αθήνα, 22 απριλίου 2025 you will encounter this pattern in court filings, ministerial decisions, and other official pdfs converted to text. the technique below extracts it: a regex matches "αθήνα," followed by a day number, a greek month name, and a four-digit year, and a dictionary maps the month name to its number. one cleaning step is always required first: replace non-breaking spaces ( \xa0 ) with regular spaces. greek government documents often contain \xa0 characters from old word processors, and without this step the regex fails silently on many pages. python · try it import re def parse_date(text): months = { "ιανουαρίου": 1, "φεβρουαρίου": 2, "μαρτίου": 3, "απριλίου": 4, "μαΐου": 5, "ιουνίου": 6, "ιουλίου": 7, "αυγούστου": 8, "σεπτεμβρίου": 9, "οκτωβρίου": 10, "νοεμβρίου": 11, "δεκεμβρίου": 12, } match = re.search(r"αθήνα,\s+(\d+\s+\w+\s+\d{4})", text) if not match: return "" day, month_text, year = match.group(1).split() month = months.get(month_text, "") return f"{day}/{month}/{year}" if month else "" # simulate text extracted from detail pages (\xa0 = non-breaking space) sample_texts = [ "ελληνικη\xa0δημοκρατια
αθήνα, 22 απριλίου 2025
ημερησια διαταξη", "ελληνικη δημοκρατια
αθήνα, 3 ιανουαρίου 2024
ημερησια διαταξη", "ελληνικη δημοκρατια
αθήνα, 15 νοεμβρίου 2023
ημερησια διαταξη", "ελληνικη δημοκρατια
αθήνα, 1 μαΐου 2025
ημερησια διαταξη", ] for raw_text in sample_texts: clean_text = raw_text.replace("\xa0", " ") date = parse_date(clean_text) header_line = [l for l in clean_text.split("
") if "αθήνα" in l][0].strip() print(f"input : {header_line}") print(f"output: {date}") print() run clear why \s+ instead of a single space? the regex uses \s+ (one or more whitespace characters) between each part of the date. government documents are inconsistently spaced — sometimes a single space, sometimes two or three. \s+ handles all of them.

chapter: Chapter Nine — Full Data Journalism Project
text: part five the complete 74-page scraper now put it all together. the strategy, in order: build the list-page url using the page number and fetch it. find div.pagecontent , then the ul , then all li elements. for each item: read the date directly from the link text, extract the lpr_id with regex, build the detail url. fetch the detail page and extract its full text content. append a dictionary with all fields to all_records . sleep 0.5 s between detail requests. print progress after each list page. sleep 1 s before the next list page. after all 74 pages: build a dataframe and save to csv. try/except blocks appear at two levels: one wraps each list-page request so a single failed page does not stop the entire run, and another wraps each detail-page request so a single failed item does not lose the rest of the list. python · copy to your notebook import requests # the library that sends http requests to websites import re # the library for searching text with patterns (regular expressions) import time # the library that lets us pause between requests from bs4 import beautifulsoup # the library that reads and searches html import pandas as pd # the library that stores data in tables and saves csv files base_url = "https://www.hellenicparliament.gr" # the website's root address list_path = "/nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan" # the path to the daily plans section total_pages = 74 # how many list pages we want to scrape headers = { # these headers make our request look like a real browser visit "accept": ( "text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9," "image/avif,image/webp,image/apng,*/*;q=0.8," "application/signed-exchange;v=b3;q=0.7" ), "accept-language": "en-us,en;q=0.9,el;q=0.8", # tell the server we understand greek and english "cache-control": "max-age=0", # ask for a fresh copy, not a cached one "referer": "https://www.hellenicparliament.gr/nomothetiko-ergo/syzitiseis-kai-psifisi", # tell the server which page we came from "sec-ch-ua": '"google chrome";v="147", "not.a/brand";v="8", "chromium";v="147"', # browser brand and version "sec-ch-ua-mobile": "?0", # we are not on a mobile device "sec-ch-ua-platform": '"macos"', # our operating system "sec-fetch-dest": "document", # we are requesting a full html page "sec-fetch-mode": "navigate", # this is a normal navigation, not a background script "sec-fetch-site": "same-origin", # the request comes from the same website "sec-fetch-user": "?1", # a real user triggered this request "upgrade-insecure-requests": "1", # redirect http to https if needed "user-agent": ( # identify ourselves as a chrome browser on macos "mozilla/5.0 (macintosh; intel mac os x 10_15_7) " "applewebkit/537.36 (khtml, like gecko) " "chrome/147.0.0.0 safari/537.36" ), } all_records = [] # empty list — we will add one dictionary per daily plan here for page_number in range(1, total_pages + 1): # loop through page numbers 1 to 74 list_url = f"{base_url}{list_path}?more=true&pageno={page_number}" # build the url for this list page try: # try everything below — if it fails, jump to except at the bottom response = requests.get(list_url, headers=headers, timeout=30) # send the request and wait up to 30 seconds if response.status_code != 200: # if the server did not return a successful response (200 = ok) print(f"page {page_number}: status {response.status_code} — skipping.") # print the error code continue # skip this page and move on to the next one soup = beautifulsoup(response.content, "html.parser") # parse the html bytes into a searchable object container = soup.find("div", class_="pagecontent") # find the main content div by its css class ul = container.find("ul") if container else none # find the list inside the div if not ul: # if there is no list on this page, something is wrong print(f"page {page_number}: no list found — stopping.") break # stop the entire loop items = ul.find_all("li") # collect every list item inside the ul page_count = 0 # counter to track how many items we scrape on this page for item in items: # loop through each list item one by one a = item.find("a") # find the link tag inside this list item if not a: # if there is no link tag, skip this item continue date = a.get_text(strip=true) # the link text is the date, already formatted as dd/mm/yyyy href = a.get("href", "") # get the href attribute — the relative url of the detail page id_match = re.search(r"lpr_id=([\w-]+)", href) # search the href for the unique session id if not id_match: # if no id was found in the href, skip this item continue lpr_id = id_match.group(1) # extract the id value — group(1) is what is inside the parentheses detail_url = f"{base_url}{list_path}?lpr_id={lpr_id}" # build the full url of the detail page try: # try to fetch the detail page detail_resp = requests.get(detail_url, headers=headers, timeout=30) # send the request if detail_resp.status_code != 200: # if the detail page did not load successfully print(f" detail {lpr_id}: status {detail_resp.status_code} — skipping.") # print the error code content = "" # store an empty string and continue else: detail_soup = beautifulsoup(detail_resp.content, "html.parser") # parse the detail page html detail_container = detail_soup.find("div", class_="pagecontent") # find the content div # extract all text and replace non-breaking spaces (\xa0) with normal spaces content = detail_container.get_text(" ", strip=true).replace("\xa0", " ") if detail_container else "" except exception: # if the request fails entirely (timeout, connection error, etc.) content = "" # store an empty string — don't crash the whole run all_records.append({ # add a new dictionary (one row) to our list "page": page_number, # which list page this record came from "date": date, # the session date in dd/mm/yyyy format "lpr_id": lpr_id, # the unique id of this daily plan document "url": detail_url, # the direct link to the full document "content": content, # the full text of the agenda }) page_count += 1 # count this item as successfully scraped time.sleep(0.5) # wait half a second before the next detail request — be polite to the server print(f"page {page_number:>2}/{total_pages}: {page_count} items (total so far: {len(all_records)})") time.sleep(1) # wait one full second before requesting the next list page except exception as e: # if the list page request itself fails completely print(f"page {page_number}: error — {e}") # print what went wrong and keep going df = pd.dataframe(all_records) # convert the list of dictionaries into a pandas dataframe (a table) df.to_csv("parliament_daily_plans.csv", index=false, encoding="utf-8-sig") # save to csv; utf-8-sig makes greek readable in excel print(f"\ndone. {len(df)} rows saved to parliament_daily_plans.csv") # final confirmation copy the csv is saved with encoding="utf-8-sig" . the bom prefix this adds tells microsoft excel to open the file as utf-8 automatically, so greek characters display correctly without any manual import step. what this scraper produces: a csv with one row per daily plan — list-page number, session date (already dd/mm/yyyy from the list), unique id, direct url, and the full text of the agenda document. at roughly 10–20 items per page across 74 pages, expect 700–1 400 rows covering years of parliamentary sessions — a complete, structured archive built with the same tools you used throughout this book.

chapter: Chapter Ten — Playwright — Scraping Pages That Need a Real Browser
partTitle: The Problem — What requests Cannot See
text: part one the problem — what requests cannot see w hen you call requests.get(url) , the server sends back the raw html file — exactly what was written on the server, before any javascript runs. on simple sites like news feeds, that raw html already contains all the content. but many modern sites work differently: they send a nearly empty html file and then use javascript, running in your browser, to fetch the data and build the page on the spot. requests never runs that javascript. it gets the empty shell and stops there. run the cell below to see what that looks like in practice. python · try it from bs4 import beautifulsoup # imagine this is the raw html that requests returns from a javascript-heavy page # the browser would show a full table of results — but requests sees this empty shell raw_html = """ """ soup = beautifulsoup(raw_html, "html.parser") # parse the html results = soup.find("div", id="results") # try to find the results container print("text inside the div:", repr(results.get_text(strip=true))) print() print("empty — because requests got the page before javascript ran.") run clear this is not a bug in your code. it is simply how those websites work. the fix is to use a tool that actually opens a browser, lets the javascript run, and only then reads the html. how to tell if a page needs a browser: open the page in chrome, then right-click → view page source. if the source looks almost empty while the page looks full, the content is being added by javascript and requests will not see it.

chapter: Chapter Ten — Playwright — Scraping Pages That Need a Real Browser
text: part two how playwright works playwright is a python library that controls a real browser — chromium, firefox, or webkit — from your code. you write python, playwright opens the browser, navigates to the url, waits for the javascript to finish, and then gives you the full rendered html. from that point you use beautifulsoup exactly as before. the key difference from requests : requests: sends one http request, gets the raw html back, done. fast, but blind to javascript. playwright: launches a full browser, loads the page, runs all the javascript, waits for everything to settle. slower, but sees exactly what a human user would see. playwright also handles cookies, sessions, and login flows automatically — because it is a real browser. you never need to set sec-fetch-* headers or worry about bot detection: the browser sends all of that for you. when to use playwright instead of requests: use requests first — it is much faster. switch to playwright only when the page content is missing or empty after a successful request.

chapter: Chapter Ten — Playwright — Scraping Pages That Need a Real Browser
text: part three installing playwright playwright needs two installation steps: first install the python library, then download the browser it will control. run these two commands in your terminal or, if you are using google colab, in a code cell with a ! at the start. terminal · or colab cell pip install playwright # install the playwright python library playwright install chromium # download the chromium browser that playwright will control copy if you are on google colab , add a ! before each line so they run as terminal commands: google colab · run in a code cell !pip install playwright # install the library !playwright install chromium # download the browser !playwright install-deps chromium # install system dependencies colab needs copy you only need to install once. after the first install, your notebook can import playwright in every session without re-running these commands — unless you are on colab, where the environment resets each session.

chapter: Chapter Ten — Playwright — Scraping Pages That Need a Real Browser
text: part four your first playwright script the pattern is always the same: start playwright, launch a browser, open a tab, navigate to a url, wait for the page to load, get the html, close the browser. the async with block makes sure the browser closes cleanly even if something goes wrong. colab and jupyter already run inside an asynchronous event loop, so we must use the async version of playwright. the only visible difference from regular python is async with instead of with , and the word await before every playwright call. await simply means: pause here and wait for this to finish before moving on. python · copy to your notebook from playwright.async_api import async_playwright # import the async version — required in colab/jupyter from bs4 import beautifulsoup # import beautifulsoup to parse the html we get back async with async_playwright() as p: # "async with" starts playwright; browser closes automatically when the block ends browser = await p.chromium.launch(headless=true) # "await" means: wait for this to finish before continuing page = await browser.new_page() # wait for a new browser tab to open await page.goto("https://www.hellenicparliament.gr/nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan?more=true&pageno=1") # navigate to the url await page.wait_for_load_state("networkidle") # wait until the page has finished all network activity html = await page.content() # get the full html of the page after javascript has run await browser.close() # close the browser # now use beautifulsoup on the rendered html, exactly as before soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # parse the html into a searchable object container = soup.find("div", class_="pagecontent") # find the main content div ul = container.find("ul") if container else none # find the list inside it if ul: items = ul.find_all("li") # collect all list items print(f"found {len(items)} items on page 1") # print how many we found for item in items[:3]: # print the first three as a preview a = item.find("a") # find the link inside the item print(a.get_text(strip=true), "→", a.get("href", "")) # print the date and href else: print("no list found.") copy notice that there are no headers to set. because playwright uses a real browser, the browser sends all the correct headers automatically — including the sec-fetch-* values that government sites check for. headless=false opens a visible browser window so you can watch the scraper work. useful for debugging. change it back to true for your final run.

chapter: Chapter Ten — Playwright — Scraping Pages That Need a Real Browser
partTitle: The Full Parliament Scraper with Playwright
text: part five the full parliament scraper with playwright this is the complete version of the chapter 9 scraper, rewritten to use playwright instead of requests . the structure is identical — only the part that fetches the html changes. beautifulsoup, regex, pandas, and csv export are exactly the same. one browser session handles all 74 list pages and all their detail pages. you do not need to open and close the browser for each request — just navigate the same tab to each url in turn. python · copy to your notebook from playwright.async_api import async_playwright # import the async version — required in colab/jupyter from bs4 import beautifulsoup # the library that reads and searches html import re # the library for searching text with patterns import asyncio # needed for asyncio.sleep() — the async-safe way to pause import pandas as pd # the library that stores data in tables and saves csv files base_url = "https://www.hellenicparliament.gr" # the website's root address list_path = "/nomothetiko-ergo/dailyplan" # the path to the daily plans section total_pages = 74 # how many list pages we want to scrape all_records = [] # empty list — we will add one dictionary per daily plan here async with async_playwright() as p: # "async with" starts playwright — same browser session for everything browser = await p.chromium.launch(headless=true) # wait for chromium to launch with no visible window tab = await browser.new_page() # wait for a browser tab to open — we reuse this same tab for every url for page_number in range(1, total_pages + 1): # loop through page numbers 1 to 74 list_url = f"{base_url}{list_path}?more=true&pageno={page_number}" # build the list page url await tab.goto(list_url) # wait for the browser to navigate to this url await tab.wait_for_load_state("networkidle") # wait until javascript has finished loading html = await tab.content() # wait for the html, then store it soup = beautifulsoup(html, "html.parser") # parse the html into a searchable object container = soup.find("div", class_="pagecontent") # find the main content div ul = container.find("ul") if container else none # find the list inside it if not ul: # if there is no list on this page, something is wrong print(f"page {page_number}: no list found — stopping.") break # stop the loop items = ul.find_all("li") # collect every list item page_count = 0 # counter for items on this page for item in items: # loop through each list item one by one a = item.find("a") # find the link tag inside this item if not a: # skip if there is no link continue date = a.get_text(strip=true) # the link text is the session date, already dd/mm/yyyy href = a.get("href", "") # get the href — the relative url of the detail page id_match = re.search(r"lpr_id=([\w-]+)", href) # search the href for the unique session id if not id_match: # skip if no id was found continue lpr_id = id_match.group(1) # extract the id — group(1) is what is inside the parentheses detail_url = f"{base_url}{list_path}?lpr_id={lpr_id}" # build the full url of the detail page await tab.goto(detail_url) # wait for the browser to navigate to the detail page await tab.wait_for_load_state("networkidle") # wait for it to fully load detail_html = await tab.content() # get the rendered html of the detail page detail_soup = beautifulsoup(detail_html, "html.parser") # parse the detail page html detail_container = detail_soup.find("div", class_="pagecontent") # find the content div # extract all text and replace non-breaking spaces (\xa0) with normal spaces content = detail_container.get_text(" ", strip=true).replace("\xa0", " ") if detail_container else "" all_records.append({ # add a new row to our list "page": page_number, # which list page this record came from "date": date, # the session date in dd/mm/yyyy format "lpr_id": lpr_id, # the unique id of this daily plan document "url": detail_url, # the direct link to the full document "content": content, # the full text of the agenda }) page_count += 1 # count this item as successfully scraped await asyncio.sleep(0.5) # async version of time.sleep() — always use this inside async code print(f"page {page_number:>2}/{total_pages}: {page_count} items (total so far: {len(all_records)})") await asyncio.sleep(1) # wait one second before the next list page await browser.close() # wait for the browser to close cleanly df = pd.dataframe(all_records) # convert the list of dictionaries into a pandas dataframe (a table) df.to_csv("parliament_playwright.csv", index=false, encoding="utf-8-sig") # save to csv; utf-8-sig makes greek readable in excel print(f"\ndone. {len(df)} rows saved to parliament_playwright.csv") # final confirmation copy playwright is slower than requests because it launches a real browser and waits for javascript on every page. for 74 list pages with ~10 detail pages each, expect the full run to take 20–40 minutes. that is the trade-off: reliability over speed.

chapter: Chapter One — What is an API?
text: part one the waiter analogy i magine you are sitting in a restaurant. you don't walk into the kitchen to get your food. instead, you tell the waiter what you want — the waiter goes to the kitchen, the kitchen prepares the meal, and the waiter brings it back to your table. an api works exactly the same way. your program is the customer. the api is the waiter. the server or database is the kitchen. you ask for something specific, the kitchen prepares the answer, and the waiter delivers it — in a format your program can read. api stands for application programming interface . the word "interface" is the important one. it is a defined contract: you ask in a specific way, and you always get the answer in a specific, predictable format. you never have to know what is happening inside the kitchen. api vs web scraping: when you scraped kathimerini in book 5, you fetched an html page designed for a human browser, then picked through the markup to find what you needed. an api skips all that. the server sends you structured data directly — no html, no parsing tags, no fragile selectors.

chapter: Chapter One — What is an API?
text: part two anatomy of an api request an api request is just a url. you visit it with your browser — or your python program — and the server responds with data. let's look at a real one. diavgeia is the greek government's online transparency registry. every official government decision — contracts, appointments, spending — must be published there by law. diavgeia provides a public api so that anyone can query this data programmatically, without an account and without any special permission. a search request to the diavgeia api looks like this: https://diavgeia.gov.gr/luminapi/api/search?q=subject:"κλιματισμός"&size=5 let's break it into its parts: part example what it means base url https://diavgeia.gov.gr/luminapi/api/ the address of the api server endpoint search the specific action you are requesting — here, a search ? ? separates the endpoint from the parameters parameter q=subject:"κλιματισμός" the search query — diavgeia expects a field name ( subject ) followed by the search term in quotes & & separates multiple parameters parameter size=5 how many results to return you can think of the endpoint as the waiter's notepad category ("i'd like something from the seafood section"), and the parameters as your specific order ("grilled, medium, no lemon"). the q parameter has its own mini-syntax: diavgeia's search engine expects you to name the field you are searching inside. subject:"κλιματισμός" searches the decision title. q:"κλιματισμός" does a general full-text search. ada:"6λωζ7λξ-φψυ" looks up a specific decision by its unique code. you will see all three patterns used in practice. try it in your browser: apis that don't require authentication can be called directly from the browser address bar. paste https://diavgeia.gov.gr/luminapi/api/search?q=subject:"κλιματισμός"&size=3 into a new tab. you will see the raw response — a wall of structured text called json.

chapter: Chapter One — What is an API?
text: part three the response — json when the api server answers, it doesn't send html. it sends a structured text format called json — javascript object notation. despite the name, json is language-agnostic: python, javascript, r, and most other programming languages can read it natively. here is a small, realistic piece of what the diavgeia api returns for a search request: { "info": { "total": 1842 }, "decisions": [ { "ada": "ψφδδ46μτλρ-αως", "subject": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης για την προμήθεια κλιματιστικών μονάδων", "issuedate": "2025-06-10", "submissiontimestamp": "2025-06-11t09:15:00.000+0300", "documenturl": "https://diavgeia.gov.gr/doc/ψφδδ46μτλρ-αως", "documenttype": "pdf", "organization": { "label": "δημος αθηναιων" }, "decisiontype": { "label": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης" } } ] } this might look unfamiliar at first, but you already know most of the patterns. json is built from two structures: json structure python equivalent how to recognise it object dict curly braces { } with "key": value pairs array list square brackets [ ] with comma-separated values string str always double-quoted: "like this" number int / float no quotes: 1842 , 14500.00 boolean bool true / false (lowercase in json) null none null when python reads json, it converts each of those structures automatically to the python equivalent. a json object becomes a dict . a json array becomes a list . a json string becomes a str . you don't have to do anything special — python handles the conversion for you.

chapter: Chapter One — What is an API?
text: part four reading json with python python's built-in json module converts a json string into a python object with a single function call: json.loads() . the s in loads stands for "string" — you are loading from a string. python · try it import json raw = '{"total": 1842, "size": 5, "page": 0}' data = json.loads(raw) print(type(data)) print(data) print(data["total"]) run clear the json string becomes a python dictionary. you access its values exactly as you would any other dict — with square brackets and the key name. now let's try a more realistic response — the kind diavgeia actually returns, with a nested structure and a list of decisions: python · try it import json raw = """ { "info": { "total": 1842 }, "decisions": [ { "ada": "ψφδδ46μτλρ-αως", "subject": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης για την προμήθεια κλιματιστικών μονάδων", "issuedate": "2025-06-10", "organization": {"label": "δημος αθηναιων"}, "decisiontype": {"label": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης"} }, { "ada": "6λωζ7λξ-φψυ", "subject": "σύμβαση προμήθειας και εγκατάστασης κλιματιστικών", "issuedate": "2025-06-08", "organization": {"label": "δημος θεσσαλονικης"}, "decisiontype": {"label": "σύμβαση"} } ] } """ data = json.loads(raw) print("total results:", data["info"]["total"]) print("decisions in this page:", len(data["decisions"])) print() for decision in data["decisions"]: print(decision["issuedate"], "|", decision["organization"]["label"]) print(" ", decision["subject"]) print() run clear notice two things about that code. first, data["info"]["total"] — you chain square brackets to navigate into nested objects. the same works for decision["organization"]["label"] : organization is itself a dict with a label key inside it. second, data["decisions"] is a python list, so you can loop over it with for exactly as you would any other list. json.loads() vs json.load(): json.loads() parses a string. json.load() (no s) reads from a file object. you will use loads() when you get the response from an api, and load() if you ever open a saved .json file from disk.

chapter: Chapter One — What is an API?
text: part five your turn — navigate the response the cell below contains a diavgeia-style response with three decisions. your task: loop through them and print the ada code, the organisation name, and the decision type label for each one. the organisation name and decision type label are nested one level deep — use the bracket-chaining technique you just saw. python · your turn import json raw = """ { "info": {"total": 3}, "decisions": [ { "ada": "ψφδδ46μτλρ-αως", "subject": "προμήθεια κλιματιστικών μονάδων", "issuedate": "2025-06-10", "organization": {"label": "δημος αθηναιων"}, "decisiontype": {"label": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης"} }, { "ada": "6λωζ7λξ-φψυ", "subject": "εγκατάσταση κλιματιστικών συστημάτων", "issuedate": "2025-06-08", "organization": {"label": "δημος θεσσαλονικης"}, "decisiontype": {"label": "σύμβαση"} }, { "ada": "ωβκ746μφωρ-πδγ", "subject": "συντήρηση κλιματισμού δημοτικών κτιρίων", "issuedate": "2025-06-05", "organization": {"label": "δημος πειραιως"}, "decisiontype": {"label": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης"} } ] } """ data = json.loads(raw) # for each decision, print: ada | organization label | decision type label for decision in data["decisions"]: print(decision["ada"], "|", decision["organization"]["label"], "|", decision["decisiontype"]["label"]) run clear what you learned in this chapter: an api is a structured, machine-readable interface to data — like a waiter who always answers in a predictable format. an api request is a url made up of a base url, an endpoint, and parameters. the response almost always comes back as json — which python reads with json.loads() and turns into familiar dictionaries and lists. in the next chapter you will send a real request to the diavgeia api using python's requests library.

chapter: Chapter Two — Your First API Request
text: part one the requests library p ython's standard library can make http requests, but the third-party requests library is simpler and is what almost every python developer uses in practice. it handles encoding, redirects, headers, and timeouts cleanly, without the verbosity of the built-in alternatives. if you are working in a jupyter notebook or a local python environment, install it once: pip install requests or inside a notebook cell: !pip install requests then import it at the top of your script: python · copy to your notebook import requests url = "https://diavgeia.gov.gr/luminapi/api/search" params = { "q": 'subject:"κλιματισμός"', "size": 5, "page": 0, "sort": "recent", } resp = requests.get(url, params=params, headers={"accept": "application/json"}, timeout=30) resp.raise_for_status() data = resp.json() print("total decisions found:", data["info"]["total"]) print("decisions on this page:", len(data["decisions"])) copy why these cells don't run here. the browser version of the course cannot make live requests to external servers. copy any cell with a copy button into a jupyter notebook and run it there. the interactive run cells later in this chapter use hardcoded responses so you can practice in the browser.

chapter: Chapter Two — Your First API Request
text: part two the request, line by line every call to the diavgeia api follows the same four-line pattern. let's go through each line. the url the search endpoint never changes. you store it in a variable so you don't have to type the full address every time: url = "https://diavgeia.gov.gr/luminapi/api/search" the params dictionary instead of building a long url string by hand, you pass a dictionary. the requests library url-encodes the values automatically and appends them after the ? : python · copy to your notebook params = { "q": 'subject:"κλιματισμός"', "size": 10, "page": 0, "sort": "recent", } copy the four most common params in a diavgeia search: parameter example value what it does q subject:"κλιματισμός" the search expression. always includes a field name before the colon. size 10 how many results to return per page. maximum is 100. page 0 which page to return. zero-based: first page is 0, second is 1. sort recent newest first. use relative when you care about text relevance. the q parameter mini-syntax the value of q is not a plain search term — it uses a small query language. you always name the field you want to search, followed by a colon and the term in quotes: pattern searches in subject:"κλιματισμός" the decision title / subject field q:"κλιματισμός" full text of the document ada:"6λωζ7λξ-φψυ" a specific ada code q:["κλιματισμός", "ψύξη"] multiple terms (any match) the get call resp = requests.get(url, params=params, headers={"accept": "application/json"}, timeout=30) requests.get() sends the http get request and returns a response object. the two keyword arguments you should always include: headers={"accept": "application/json"} — tells the server you want json back, not html. timeout=30 — if the server takes more than 30 seconds to respond, python raises an error instead of waiting forever. raise_for_status and .json() resp.raise_for_status() data = resp.json() raise_for_status() checks the http status code. if the server returned an error — 404 not found, 500 server error — it raises a python exception immediately, so your code fails loudly rather than silently processing an empty or broken response. resp.json() parses the response body as json and returns a python dictionary. it is equivalent to writing json.loads(resp.text) , but shorter.

chapter: Chapter Two — Your First API Request
text: part three working with the response the cell below simulates a realistic diavgeia response — the same structure your code would receive after the resp.json() call. run it and examine the output, then try changing the print statements. python · try it import json # this is what resp.json() returns after a real api call raw = """ { "info": {"total": 312}, "decisions": [ { "ada": "ψφδδ46μτλρ-αως", "subject": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης για την προμήθεια κλιματιστικών μονάδων", "issuedate": "2025-06-10", "submissiontimestamp": "2025-06-11t09:15:00.000+0300", "documenturl": "https://diavgeia.gov.gr/doc/ψφδδ46μτλρ-αως", "documenttype": "pdf", "organization": {"label": "δημος αθηναιων"}, "decisiontype": {"label": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης"} }, { "ada": "6λωζ7λξ-φψυ", "subject": "σύμβαση προμήθειας και εγκατάστασης κλιματιστικών", "issuedate": "2025-06-08", "submissiontimestamp": "2025-06-09t11:30:00.000+0300", "documenturl": "https://diavgeia.gov.gr/doc/6λωζ7λξ-φψυ", "documenttype": "pdf", "organization": {"label": "δημος θεσσαλονικης"}, "decisiontype": {"label": "σύμβαση"} }, { "ada": "ωβκ746μφωρ-πδγ", "subject": "συντήρηση κλιματισμού δημοτικών κτιρίων", "issuedate": "2025-06-05", "submissiontimestamp": "2025-06-06t14:20:00.000+0300", "documenturl": "https://diavgeia.gov.gr/doc/ωβκ746μφωρ-πδγ", "documenttype": "pdf", "organization": {"label": "δημος πειραιως"}, "decisiontype": {"label": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης"} } ] } """ data = json.loads(raw) print("total decisions in database:", data["info"]["total"]) print("decisions on this page:", len(data["decisions"])) print() for decision in data["decisions"]: print(decision["ada"]) print(" subject :", decision["subject"]) print(" date :", decision["issuedate"]) print(" org :", decision["organization"]["label"]) print(" type :", decision["decisiontype"]["label"]) print(" pdf :", decision["documenturl"]) print() run clear notice that data["info"]["total"] is 312 — that is the full count of matching decisions in the database — while len(data["decisions"]) is only 3, because that is what one page returns. pagination is how you collect all 312, and you will do exactly that in a later chapter.

chapter: Chapter Two — Your First API Request
text: part four the safe extraction pattern real api responses are not always complete. a field that exists for one decision may be null — python's none — for another. if you access decision["organization"]["label"] and organization is none , python raises a typeerror and your script stops. the standard defence is two steps. first, use .get() with a fallback. second, guard the nested access with or {} : python · try it import json # one of these decisions is missing the organization field entirely raw = """ { "info": {"total": 2}, "decisions": [ { "ada": "ψφδδ46μτλρ-αως", "subject": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης για κλιματιστικά", "issuedate": "2025-06-10", "organization": {"label": "δημος αθηναιων"}, "decisiontype": {"label": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης"}, "documenturl": "https://diavgeia.gov.gr/doc/ψφδδ46μτλρ-αως" }, { "ada": "6λωζ7λξ-φψυ", "subject": "σύμβαση εγκατάστασης κλιματιστικών", "issuedate": "2025-06-08", "organization": null, "decisiontype": null, "documenturl": null } ] } """ data = json.loads(raw) for decision in data["decisions"]: organization = decision.get("organization") or {} decision_type = decision.get("decisiontype") or {} row = { "ada": decision.get("ada", ""), "subject": decision.get("subject", ""), "issuedate": decision.get("issuedate", ""), "documenturl": decision.get("documenturl", ""), "organization": organization.get("label", ""), "decisiontype": decision_type.get("label", ""), } print(row) run clear the expression decision.get("organization") or {} reads as: "get the value of organization , and if it is none or missing, use an empty dictionary instead." then {}.get("label", "") safely returns an empty string. your loop never crashes, and every row in your dataset has consistent keys. build a dict for each decision. once you have a row dictionary like the one above, you can collect all of them in a list and pass that list directly to pandas.dataframe() . that step — api → list of dicts → dataframe — is the full pipeline you will build in the next chapter.

chapter: Chapter Two — Your First API Request
text: part five filtering with fq the q parameter searches across all decisions. the fq parameter filters the results — it narrows the search to a specific organisation, decision type, or date range without affecting the relevance ranking. to filter by organisation, you need the organisation's numeric uid. you can find it by searching diavgeia's web interface and reading the url, or by looking it up in the discovery endpoints listed in the cheat sheet. once you have it, add it as a second key in your params dict: python · copy to your notebook import requests url = "https://diavgeia.gov.gr/luminapi/api/search" params = { "q": 'subject:"κλιματισμός"', "fq": 'organizationuid:"6167"', # limit to one organisation "sort": "recent", "page": 0, "size": 20, } resp = requests.get(url, params=params, headers={"accept": "application/json"}, timeout=30) resp.raise_for_status() data = resp.json() for decision in data.get("decisions", []): org = (decision.get("organization") or {}).get("label", "") print(decision.get("ada"), "|", org) print(" ", decision.get("subject")) print() copy you can also filter by date range. diavgeia uses its own timestamp syntax inside the fq value: python · copy to your notebook import requests url = "https://diavgeia.gov.gr/luminapi/api/search" params = { "q": 'subject:"κλιματισμός"', "fq": "issuedate:[dt(2024-01-01t00:00:00) to dt(2024-12-31t23:59:59)]", "sort": "recent", "page": 0, "size": 20, } resp = requests.get(url, params=params, headers={"accept": "application/json"}, timeout=30) resp.raise_for_status() data = resp.json() print("decisions in 2024:", data["info"]["total"]) copy fq can be stacked. if you need to filter by both organisation and date, you can pass a list as the value: "fq": ['organizationuid:"6167"', "issuedate:[dt(2024-01-01t00:00:00) to dt(2024-12-31t23:59:59)]"] . the requests library will send both as separate fq parameters.

chapter: Chapter Two — Your First API Request
text: part six your turn — extract and organise the cell below contains a response with four decisions. your task is to build a list of dictionaries using the safe extraction pattern, then print the total number of decisions and each row. use .get() everywhere and guard nested access with or {} . python · your turn import json raw = """ { "info": {"total": 4}, "decisions": [ { "ada": "ψφδδ46μτλρ-αως", "subject": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης για κλιματιστικά", "issuedate": "2025-06-10", "documenturl": "https://diavgeia.gov.gr/doc/ψφδδ46μτλρ-αως", "documenttype": "pdf", "organization": {"label": "δημος αθηναιων"}, "decisiontype": {"label": "ανάληψη υποχρέωσης"} }, { "ada": "6λωζ7λξ-φψυ", "subject": "σύμβαση εγκατάστασης κλιματιστικών", "issuedate": "2025-06-08", "documenturl": "https://diavgeia.gov.gr/doc/6λωζ7λξ-φψυ", "documenttype": "pdf", "organization": {"label": "δημος θεσσαλονικης"}, "decisiontype": {"label": "σύμβαση"} }, { "ada": "ωβκ746μφωρ-πδγ", "subject": "συντήρηση κλιματισμού δημοτικών κτιρίων", "issuedate": "2025-06-05", "documenturl": null, "documenttype": null, "organization": {"label": "δημος πειραιως"}, "decisiontype": null }, { "ada": "ρψξ946μτλρ-βωδ", "subject": "προμήθεια και τοποθέτηση κλιματιστικών μονάδων", "issuedate": "2025-06-01", "documenturl": "https://diavgeia.gov.gr/doc/ρψξ946μτλρ-βωδ", "documenttype": "pdf", "organization": null, "decisiontype": {"label": "σύμβαση"} } ] } """ data = json.loads(raw) rows = [] for decision in data["decisions"]: organization = decision.get("organization") or {} decision_type = decision.get("decisiontype") or {} rows.append({ "ada": decision.get("ada", ""), "subject": decision.get("subject", ""), "issuedate": decision.get("issuedate", ""), "documenturl": decision.get("documenturl", ""), "organization": organization.get("label", ""), "decisiontype": decision_type.get("label", ""), }) print("rows collected:", len(rows)) print() for row in rows: print(row) run clear what you learned in this chapter: how to call the diavgeia search endpoint with requests.get() , how to use the params dictionary to build the query, what raise_for_status() and
